LABOR (Connecticut) NEWS
LABOR ALONE IS
OPPOSING PLAN TO
CHINIFY AMERICA
"Coolies in Hawaii" Plot Would
Annul Every Anti-Peonage Law
Extant.
Washington, Aug. 12. Congress
man Cable, member of the house com
mittee on immigration, shows that it
the proposed coolie resolution is ap
proved by congress it will annul all
tederal acts to prevent slavery, peon
age, contract labor and Oriental im
migration. hentiment in congress is swinging
away troiu the coolie bill, ollcially
known as 11. J. Res. 171 introduced
by the territorial delegate lrom
Havvai.
While the danger is by no means
removed, A. h. ot L. representatives
believe that a strong sentiment ha
been built up against the measure
and that Hawaiian sugar planters
are far from certain now of the de
sired Hordes of Chinese coolies to
work on the plantations as bond
men. .
The entire tight against the pio
position has been waged by organ
ized labor. Wo other organization
has protested against the reinlroduc
tiou of slavery on American soil.
Under the terms of the joint reso
lution it would be possible upon
porclamations by the i'resident that
an emergency exists, to import ap
proximately 50,000 Chinese coolies in
uond, mto Hawaii.
During the testimony ot I resident
Gompers betore the house immigra
uon committee it was developed Uiat
about 25 years ago, when the question
ot the annexation ot Hawaii was un
der consideration, a conlerence was
held at a hotel in this city between
members of congress, the sugar inter
ests of the United Mates and repre
sentatives of Hawaii. , , . ;
At that time Claud bprccklcs in
formed the Hawaiians that it the is
lands were permitted to come into
the United Mates the people ot the
islands would have to accept the la
bor laws of the United Mates as well
as the tariff laws. They would have
to swallow the bitter with the sweet
and that they must take their chance
with the citizens of the United Mate.
The Hawaiians said that they knew
they would have to do that and would
accept the conditions.
President Gompers was on the
stand before the house immigration
committee continuously for a day
and a halt, presenting to the commit
tee the objections of organized labor
and the objections of American citi
zenship to the coolie resolution.
Inch by inch the A. b of C. execu
tive battered down every excuse tor
the importation of coolie labor, tie
showed that there was no "ssll
for coolie labor and that under the
terms of the United Mates consti
tution there could be no such con
dition of bondage as that which
would result from adoption ot H. J
Res. 171. the coolie resolution
President Gompers concluded his
testimony with an emphatic declar
ation against the r e :so Union based
upon the principles mvui.w, e
which he said: . .
"1 cal your attention to the tact
that it is not wise to take the heart
and the spirit out of Americas
workers. It is not wise to lead them
into a position of fear, anguish and
anger where no loyal support comes
because no voluntary loyal support
comes to a government that uses op
pression and suppression.
"We appeal to you, as we have
the right as men and as citizens, to
maintain the American policy ot Chi
nese exclusion by law and to make
by law the exclusion ot the Japanese
and all other orientals. In my opin
ion the people of the republic of the
United States have only just entered
upon the real mission ot" our republic
to be the leader in industrial progress,
in democratic government and hu
manitarian ideals. Do not let us de
part from that policy, sound, good
and progressive, but, rather, strength
cn it "
Congressman John L. Cable, of
Ohio, member of the committee on
immigration, compiled the laws that
would be destroyed if the coohe reso
lution is adopted, including the con
stitutional provision which prohibits
involuntary servitude except as pun
ishment for crime. .
The Ohio lawmaker showed that m
specific instances United States
statute law. designed to prevent peon
age ,to restrict Oriental immigration,
to eradicate slavery, to prohibit con
tract labor, to exclude Chinese from
Hawaii, and in general to safeguard
the rights and freedom of men lrom
the imposition of involuntary servi
tude, would be nullified by the pro
posed coolie resolution.
This tabulation sustained state
ments made by I'resident Gompers
and corroborated his declarations on
the specific point that to enact the
proposed resolution would reverse
the policy which the American gov
ernment has maintained during 40
years.
The house committee an immigra
tion has not announced its intention
regarding future hearings, but it is
understood that hearings are planned
by the senate committee on immigra
tion. "BIG SIX" SIGNS
WITH PUBLISHERS
Newspapers Agree to Old Wage
Scale and Present Hours
of Labor.
New York, Aug. 12. Typograph
ical Union No. 6, known as "Big Six,
has ratified an agreement with the
Newspaper Publishers' Association
here by which existing wages and
hours are continued without chatige.
The agreement, however, specifies
that the printers shall work on Satur
days for straight time for eight hours,
instead of receiving time and a halt
for the first three hours, as has been
the custom. This provision applies
to the printers employed on the Sun
day morning newspapers, who under
the old system came to work at 3
o'clock in the afternoon and were
paid an extra rate from then to 6
o'clock, after which they worked eight
more hours usually, at the regular
rate. Under the new rule the night
shift will go to work at 4 o'clock
and work for eight hours at the regu
lar rate, receiving time and a halt for
work in excess of the eight hours.
The present wage, which is con
tinued, for newspaper printers is $55
a week for the day shift of 45 hours,
$58 a week for the night shift of 45
hours, and $61 a week for the mid
night shift of 42 hours. The new
agreement, which runs until June 30,
1922, will effect about 2,300 printers.
MACKENZIE NOW
IN FULL CHARGE
OF PROHIBITION
MacAuliffe to Be Under New State
Director and State
Divided.
With the assumption of office of pro
hibition director for Connecticut by
Harry H. Mackenzie of Bethel, also
conies the expected change this week of
the conduct of the office and hereafter
Thomas MacAuliffe, prohibition en
forcement agent, will be under Mr. Mac
kenzie instead of the Boston office of
the enforcement bureau. Simultaneously
the work will he subdivided into dis
tricts, called held divisions.
Under McAulifie, the large force of
federation prohilvition agents will be di-
ided into two groups, the head of one
Deing located in Willimantic and the head
of the other in Bridgeport. These will
be exclusive of the two guards maintain
ed by the government at the Warehouse
Point distillery.
There will be an executive divisioi
head working directly under Director
Mackenzie in the office. In addition to
Mr. Mackenzie's own secretary there
will he one clerk in the office to work on
applications, permits and withdrawals,
one for mails and tiles and one for revo
cations and reports. The field force will
also he allowed a clerk.
ENGLISH LABOR
CONFERENCE FAILS
Delegates Appointed Two Years
Ago Resigning From
Futile Task.
London. England.. Aug. 12. The big
industrial conference, called by the gov
ernment more than two years ago. seems
to have failed, and representatives of the
cmplovers and workers on the joint com
mittee of the national industrial council
are resigning.
The committee was formed in Febru
arv, 1919. and two months after issued a
report that was unanimously approved by
the national industrial council and ac
cepted in principle by the government.
Nothing , has happened since that time
and one 'paper states that "it is not easy
for a body of men to keep their self re
spect under such circumstances."
When this movement was launched it
will agreed that the national industrial
council would consider and advise the
government on industrial questions. The
government declared its willingness to
proceed at once with the legislative and
other steps necessary to give effect to the
reports, but a coldness on the part of
government officials has appeared and
nothing has been done with the rf com
mendations. With the members of the board re
Hgning it is only a question of a short
time until the whole structure is in ruins.
PROFITEERS PLAN
TO ESCAPE TAXES
ON WARTIME LOOT
Levy Now on Extreme Wealthy to
Be Shifted to
Others.
Washington. Aug. 12. Will the ex
treme wealthy in this country escape the
tax burden so heavily borne by others?
Will the tax now leview on the extreme
wealthy be shifted to others?
And will the war profiteers escape with
their loot?
A highly organized propaganda to
ohilsli excess profits and reduce the sur
tax feature of the income tax law has
been conducted. The reduction of the
surtax or the cutting down of the ad
ditional income tax on large incomes
will affect but a few thousand people.
This group, while small, represents the
plutocracy of our country.
Organized labor demands that the ex
cess profits and surtaxes be retained.
It is stated that administration
leaders have agreed to abolish the ex
cess profits tax. dating back to the first
of the vear. This means a loss of half
a billion dollars. Another $90,000,000
will he lost through reducing the surtaxes
and $50,000,000 will be lost by repealing
the nuisance tax and the tax on luxury
clothing.
To offset this loss it is proposed to
increase the income tax on all corpora
tions. It is claimed that large corpora
tions will profit by this plan at the cost
of small business. Drastic cuts in ex
penditures are also proposed.
Numerous kites are sent up to sound
public opinion on additional taxes that
are given the soothing term, "tax read
justments." One kite will tell us that
all checks will be taxed, but this "feeler"
brings such a storm of protest that the
kite falls to the ground.
The next day another kite is sent up
to find out how a tax of $10 on automo
biles will "take." The plan is to assess
every auto a like amount, whether it be
flivver or touring car. The roars of
protest drives this kite into cold storage.
No one seems willing to assume the
burden that is intended to be shifted
from the backs of the wealthy and war
profiteers.
Organized labor is urging Congress
man Keller's plan to tox land values on
the millions of acres of land held out of
use by large corporations and land-owning
interests. This plan borders on trea
son to the patriots who want excess tax
diluted. They don't want their land hold
ings taxed no more than their war loot.
In addition the railroads want $500,
000.000 that the government owes them
while they insist that the millions they
owe the government be overlooked for a
few years. The railroads want the gov
ernment to O. K. bonds for this amount.
1 he shipping board would be thankful
for a couple of hundred millions, while
other millions are being called for bv the
army, navy and ether defensive pro
cesses. About t he only thing that seems cer
tain is that the "little fellow" will be
lucky if he escapes as he is.
The situation is distracting some con
gressmen, who have in mind next year's
elections. They want to favor the profi
teers and they fear the wrath of the vast
majority who demand that the war loot
be taxed. The situation may result in
the following ad appearing in the daily
press :
"'Wanted One competent navigator
who can chart a safe political route on
the troublesome taxation sea. Wages no
object if party makes good. Apply at
national capitol."
CARMEN CONVENE
IN CANADA, REPORT
300 PER CENT. GAIN
Railway Workers Make Phenomenal
Increase Since Last
Convention.
The 14th general convention of the
Brotherhood of Railway Carmen of
America opened in Toronto, Canada,
on Monday. Credentials were sent in
from approximately 1,500 delegates
coming from as far south as the Pan
ama Canal Zone, north to Alaska,
and all way points. The Loyal Star,
the women's auxiliary to the brother
hood, held its sixth convention at the
same time and place, with about 250
delegates in attendance.
The Carmen meet in general con
vention every four years, the last be
ing held at Forth Worth, Texas, in
191 7. In these four years the mem
bership has increased from 50,000 to
200,000, or 300 per cent., a record per
haps never achieved by any other la
bor organization.
Of paramount importance to the
delegates and the entire membership
is the order of the Railroad Labor
Board reducing wages and the pend
ing conferences for negotiating agree
ments on rules and working con
ditions. This will be considered and
acted upon by the convention-.
Other matters will be the purchase
or erection of a home office building
in Kansas City and the advisability
of the brotherhood's establishing a
co-operative national bank along the
lines of the Engineers' bank in Cleve
land. A large number of resolutions
and proposed amendments to the
laws of the Brotherhood have been
prepared by individual members and
lodges and printed in the Carmen's
Journal, will be formally considered.
Among those who addressed the
convention on important subjects
were Samuel Gompers, president,
American Federation of Labor; B. M.
Jewell, president, Railway Employes'
"Department, A. F. of L.; Wan en S.
Stone, grand chief, Brotherhood of
Locomotive Engineers; William H.
Johnson, president, International As
sociation of Machinists; Edward
Keating, manager and editor of La
bor, and Glenn E. Plumb, apostle of
industrial democracy.
The Brotherhood of Railway Car
men was organized at Topeka, Kan.,
in September, 1890, two brothers,
Frank L. and W. H. Rouemus being
the leaders in the movement, but the
organization was shot to pieces dur
ing the A. R. U. strike of 1894, com
ing out of the conflict with only a few
local lodges holding charters. The
real development of the brotherhood
began at the Atlanta convention in
1909, when Martin F. Ryan, a young
and aggressive leader, was chosen
general president. From then on the
brotherhood took a place among the
militant labor organizations of the
country.
When Ryan was elected president
12 vcars ago the brotherhood had a
total membership of 17,534. divided
among a comparatively few scattered
lodges. It was $150,000 in debt and
had only $1,200 in its treasury. To
day the" brotherhood has 1,564 local
lodges, 200,000 members, all debts
paid, and $2,968,953.26 in the treasury.
From a non-resistent policy the
brotherhood has become a fighting
organization, fightii? for the rights
of its members. This new policy has
decreased the daily hours of labor of
its members from 10, 11, and 12 to
eight, and increased their pay enve
lopes on an average 100 per cent. The
Brotherhood Railway Carmen of
America presents one of the best evi
dences of the value of organization to
wage workers.
BUY ONLY UNION-MADE GOODS
Food Prices Jump
Again During July
Federal Bureau Investigation Shows
Raises in 14 Principal
Cities.
Washington. Aug. 12. An investiga
tion by the United States bureau of la
bor statistics of retail food costs from
June 15 to July 15 in 14 of the principal
cities shows an increase in price in all
but three of these cities. In Butte the
increase was 6 per cent. ; in Cleveland
and Portland, Me., 5 per cent. ; in
Bridgeport, 3 per cent. ; in Houston,
Newark, New York and Washington, D.
C, 3 per cent. ; in Birmingham and in
Charleston, S. C, 1 per cent., and in
Cincinnati, two-tenths of 1 per cent.
Norfolk and Dallas reported decreases,
1 and one-tenth of 1 per cent, respective
ly. St. Louis reported no change during
the month.
BUY ONLY UNION-MADE GOODS
House of Hasselbach
Is Long Established
Noted Confectionery Store in Busi
ness on Chapel Street for
33 Years.
The most favorably known and
oldest confectionery and ice cream
store in New Haven is the House of
Hasselbach, which was established 33
years ago on Chapel street, and has
had a continuous historv at the same
I .address since the beginning of its
.... f 1 TVT TT
micti, luuiiucu uy a iew naven
man, the late William F. Hasselbach,
it very rapidly won the reputation
for the highest quality of candy and
ice cream and this reputation has been
maintained to date. At all times it
has been owned and controlled by
New Haveners. Upon the death" of
Mr. Hasselbach in 1917 the business
was continued by his brother and
sisters until 1920 when their interests
were sold to New Haven business
men and a corporation organized to
take over the business. It has been
the especial thought of the new man
agement to continue the views of the
founder in the matter of high quality
of goods and service to patrons.
As general manager for the store,
James A. DeBowes, a New Haven
man of wide crperience in hotel and
candy line, was brought from the
McAlpin -iiotel in New York. The
directors of the corporation at the
present time are all well known in
New Haven business and professional
circles and consist of John J. Mc
Keon, Arthur B. O'Keefe, Edward J.
1'oli, James A. DeBowes, and Mat
thew A. Reynolds.
The new manager has introduced
lunches which have already won the
high approval of patrons.
In accordance with the general ten
dencies of the times, the manager
has carefully considered reduction of
prices so far as is consistent with
the quality demanded by patrons and
with reasonable wages.
ALERT UNIONISTS
BLOCK REACTION IN
NATIONCONGRESS
Newly Organized Legislative Com
mittee of A. F. of L.
Right on Job.
Washington, Aug. 12. The recently
organized joint legislative committee, un
der the direction and leadership of Pres
ident (iompers, has shown its value thus
far in three important cases, where the
resources of labor were concentrated
against reactionary proposals in con
gress. The first bill provides for the abolish
ment of preliminary hearings in extradi
tion, permitting accused persons to be
spirited away without opportunity to
safeguard their rights in the place of
their domicile. The second is house
joint resolution 11, permitting the im
portation of Chinese coolies in bond in
to Hawaii, breaking down the principle
of Asiatic exclusion. The third is taxa
tion legislation, which would permit the
nation's war-made millionaires to escape
tax free with their booty and shift the
burden on consumers.
At the initial meeting of the joint
legislative committee, called by Presi
dent Gompers, 27 representatives were en
rolled, representing the A. F. of L. and
various international and subordinate
bodies. President Gompers was elected
chairman of the joint committee, with
William C. Roberts of the A. F. of L.
legislative committee as secretary.
The purpose of the conference is to
bring together trade union legislative
representatives, stationed in the nation's
capital either permanently or temporar
ily, so that their activities will be co
ordinated. This permits labor to center
a'l of its forces upon any given proposi
tion In addition it s-.rves as a channel
through which there may be a constant
flow of information concerning legisla
tion, as each representative reports at
once any situation needing attention.
This is communicated to all representa
tives, and when necessary special meet
ings will be called.
Constant inter-communication is main
tained through the office of the secretary
with regular reports made by mail and
emergency notices transmitted by tele
phone. Railroads Planning
( Continued from First Page.)
rates for overtime, are held in abey
ance The Labor News is in a position
to predict that when the answer is
given the men it will be another slash.
The four railway brotherhoods and
the Switchmen's Union are going to
be told that they must submit to the
abolition of the time and one-half rule
which required years to obtain. It is
likely that another wage cut to pile
on top of the recent reduction of I2j
per cent, will either be announced or
it will be intimiited to the men that
they can expect it in the future, but
just when will be problematical.
Thirty lines of railroads have al
ready filed requests with the United
States Railway Labor Board that they
be permitted to do away with the time
and one-half rule. The board has not
held a hearing and no date has been
set. It is the prediction that the rule
will be abrogated and the men suffer
heavily.
The general .ittitude of the roads
was shown in the conference held in
New York last week. They were ask
ed to state whether or not they would
restore wages iu effect June 21, 1920,
if all demands for further decreases
would be withdrawn and if the de
mands for the elimination of time and
one-half for overtime and radical
schedule revision will be withdrawn
and not pressed again for a stated
period.
The railway executives said that to
grant the first request would be equiv
alent to an increase in wages of 12
per cent, "when all other wages are
coming down." Jf they acceded to the
second demand they said that no fur
ther reduction would be warranted
even the cost of living showed a
heavy reduction. The chiefs of the
brotherhoods were told that if the
question of time and one-half for
overtime was eliminated it would be
taking the question out of the hands
of the Railroad Labor Board and vio
late every agreement the men have
with their respective roads.
It is known that all of the shop
crafts affiliated with the American
Federation of Iabor, as well as the
railway clerks, have taken a strike
vote and that 9.12 per cent, of them
are in favor of a strike if the drastic
wage cuts are put into effect. They
are only waiting for the railway
brotherhoods and the Switchmen's
Union to make public their stand on
the wage cuts.
The wage requests presented to
eastern railroad by the four railroad
brotherhoods and the switchmen's
union were rejerted by presidents of
the lines at a conference in New
York yesterday.
A formal statement, which failed to
give the roads' reason for refusing
the men's requeits, was issued at the
close of the conference. It read:
"The eastern presidents' conference
met today to discuss the report of its
special committee which recently
conferred with officials of the four
railroad brotherhoods and the switch
men in regard to certain questions the
qrganizations ha.ve raised.
"After giving careful consideration
to the question at issue, the confer
ence decided that the secretary should
notify the brotherhoods that condi
tions" make it impossible to grant
their requests."
PRESIDENT GOMPERS IS
INVITED TO ITALY
- Washington, Aug. 12. President
Gompers has been invited to become a
member of and attend the international
sociological congress to be held this year
in Turin. Italy, beginning October 9.
The invitation was extended by Prof.
Francesco Cosentini, director of the in
stitute of sociology of the university
of Turin.
The congress is to be "for the broad
cast discussion of problems which im
press themselves upon its minds of all
those who desire a better future for hu
manitv." DIDN'T SPLIT WORKERS.
Newark. N. J., Aug. 12. Managers of
burlesque theaters in this city have failed
to split theatrical employes by offering
a contract to musicians. The offer was
rejected. The managers are attempting
to install the anti-union shop. The stage
employes, electricians, musicians and the
other employes have formed an alliance
and will stand together.
RAIL LINES NOW
EARNING PROFITS
Returns to I. C. C. Indicate They Are
Getting Their 6
Per Cent.
Returns made by class 1 roads to the
Interstate Commerce Commission in
dicate that many of the carriers are now
earning the 6 per cent guaranteed them
by the transportation act.
This result has been obtained by starv
ing the railroads. Motive power and
equipment are in bad shape, and condi
tions are becoming progressively worse.
Managers, apparently, have sacrificed
everything to dividends, and the effect of
this unwise policy will be manifest when
industrial conditions approach .normal.
Warnings have been sounded by gov
ernment officials of a pending fuel fam
ine during the winter because the roads
will be unable to meet demands made
on transportation.
In a memorandum prepared for Pres
ident Harding by the Director General
of Railroads it is shown that on July 1
bad-order cars had reached the unheard
of percentage of 23.9, almost 14 per cent
above normal.
The percentage of bad-order locomo
tives is also 14 per cent above normal.
More than 7,000 locomotives have been
permitted to become unfit for use.
The Director General estimates de
ferred maintenance of $400,000,000, al
most exactly the amount the railroads
require to meet the six-months' dividend
requirements.
Mexico and Connecticut
(Continued from Page One.)
won't distribute them in Mexico, that is
certain.
Mr. Davison explained to Mexican of
ficials what the "'open shop" really means.
He made short work of the so-called
"American plan," pointing out that it
wasn't American at all. "But the reverse
of it. It had for its object the subjuga
tion of workers, the denial of rights that
are granted as a matter of course by
practically all other nations, and the
setting up of an industrial autocracy with
all the elements of feudalism."
"I think it is pretty generally under
stood in Mexico that those who repre
sent the 'open shop' in America are the
real enemies of Mexico," said Mr. Da
vison. "They are believed to be the same
interests that have financed revolutions
to the great injury of the people, the
same crowd that is today clamoring for
intervention, not in the interest of the
Mexican people, but in the interests of
those who have been exploiting them."
The Mexican government, Mr. Davi
son declares, feels that American public
opinion will support a movement that di
rectly helps the American people and
only indirectly benefits Mexicans. Or
ganized labor has agreed to protect
Mexico's interest by exercising super
vision over goods being manufactured for
it. thus eliminating all species of graft
and commission. That will be a definite
gain.
"But the real, the underlying thought
back of the whole proposition," he adds,
"is a conviction on the part of Mexican
authorities that they can raise the stand
ard of their own works by assisting in
maintaining the standards of workers of
a nation that will, by reason of its close
proximity, continue to exercise a great
influence upon the lives and methods of
those who reside across the border.
"Tt is an admixture of broad humani
tarianism and sound business. It is the
antithesis of the hateful policy of some
American manufacturers of getting as
much as possible, giving as little as they
are forced to give, and using force to
accomplish their purposes when other
plans fail."
DEFLATION HITS
KAR'S GREAT HERO
Sergeant Alvin Yorke, declared to be
the outstanding hero of the war, is about
to lose his farm because he is unable to
make his payments on a mortgage of
$12,500 held against his farm. He will,
however, retain his self-respect, he hav
ing steadfastly refused to capitalize his
heroism by accepting from moving pic
ture and vaudeville managers salaries
that would quickly liquidate his indebted
ness. In the Argonne on October 18, 1918,
Sergeant Yorke. captured 122 Germans,
and was awarded many medals for his
exploit.
After the war Yorke married and has
a young son. His farm was partly paid
for by a popular subscription of $11,275.
The drop in the prices of corn and pro
duce during the last year is responsible
for his present financial difficulty.
There is some considerable dispute
about Yorke's doing the alleged stunt,
records and witnesses declaring that
Sergt. John Early of New Haven actu
ally did the capturing and turned them
over to Yorke when he fell exhausted
from wounds as he got the Huns off
the battlefield.
UNION RAISES WAGES.
Leavenworth, Kan., Aug. 12.
Through organization hod carriers
have raised wages from 60 cents an
hour to 75 cents.
THE PALACE.
Having in mind the thought that
his patrons must have a good long
loud laugh every once in so often
Mr. Poli has booked for presentation
at the Palace this Sunday night what
the dyed in the wool picture review

ous big production that Mack Sennett
the comedy king has ever turned out
for the screen. It is called "A Small
Town Idol," and it measures up six
reels or 6,000 feet of film with a laugh
or a shriek in every foot of film. With
practically every member of Sennett'
all-star aggregation of funny folk en

will live up to its advance promise to
furnish melodrama that will make
you gasp and grip your chair arms,

On the same bill for Sunday night
only there will be offered an added
attraction, "Hearts and Masks," one

News.
The Palace is delightfully cool,
sanitary and comfortable at all times
and the price scale for Sunday night
is of the popular order. "A Small
Town Idol" will be on view the first
three days of the week also in con

this combination is one of the most
notable formed for vaudeville in
years. Miss Whitney is a splendid
actress on the speaking stage and
Robert Emmet Keane prior to his
marriage with Miss Whitney was
rated as the best single male enter
tainer in vaudeville being a big timer
in every sense. The special feature of
the bill will be Marie Gaspar pre
senting "Sunshadows of Songland,"
and here is another instance where
musical comedy's loss is vaudeville's
gain. Miss Gaspar is a pretty French
girl who is credited with a "wicked"
pair of shoulders. Other acts include
Copes and Hutton, two male comedi
an in a screamingly funny offering,
"Gus Jones' Tpo Darn Rough," and
Morris and Morris in "Going to the
Golf Links." The bill will be com
pleted with Brown Gardner and Gra-

title: The Connecticut labor news.

text:
LABOR (Connecticut) NEWS
Seven
COME THOUSANDS OF MILES
TO OPPOSE COOLIE LABOR
Delegation From Honolulu Labor
Bodies Visits Washington to Pro
test Admission of Chinese Workers
to Islands.
Washington, Aug. 5. If the sugar
planters of Hawaii have reached the
point where they feel they are forced
to import Chinese coolie labor, then
it is time their business was re
organized. That was the declaration made to
l'resident Ciompers, Secretary Morri
son and the A. F. of L. legislative
committee by G. V. Wright, presi
dent of the Honolulu Central Labor
body reporting at A. '. of L. head
quarters upon arrival here.
l'resident Wright and Treasurer
W. K. Chilton came to Washington
representing the Honolulu central
body to appear before the committee
on immigration of the house in pro
test against H. R. joint resolution 171,
the purpose of which is to open the
doors to coolie immigration to Ha
waii. L'nder the provision of that reso
lution it is estimated that upon
proclamation of the l'resident that an
emergency exists as many as- 50,000
Chinese coolies can be brought to
Hawaii ,and it is the expressed fear
of labor that this would, in addition
to constituting at the outset a viola
tion of a principle maintained for 40
years, be an entering wedge for im
migration of coolies to the mainland.
1'rior to the arrival of the Hawaii
an labor representatives, the officers
of the A. F. of L. protested energeti
cally and notified all Pacific and inter-mountain
central bodies of the
crucial situation. The result of this
was that a large number of protests
were lodged by wire with Congress
man Albert Johnson, chairman of the
house committee on immigration.
"At the present time," said l'resi
dent Wright, reporting to the officers
of the A. F. of L. in the office of
l'resident Gompers, "the sugar inter
ests are dominating all the commer
cial and industrial affairs in the terri
tory of Hawaii. They aie autocrats.
They have the system down so hne
that if a mechanic in any of the, trades
incurs their enmity or displeasure that
man has to leave the islands. They
have a blacklist.
"The trouble is that their planta
tions are too large. They are all con
trolled by one little group of five or
10 men with their interlocking direc
torates and trust companies of wImcIi
the directors or stockholders control
not only the plantations but the tran
sportation companies.1'
llolh l'resident Wright and Treas
urer Chilton denied emphatically the
need for importation of labor to the
islands, l'resident Wright said that
at the most the sugar companies can
not now employ more than 3,500
workers, while there are fully 2,000
unorganized unemployed in the is
lands. 14:
The whole secret of the movement
to admit Chinese coolies is, he de
clared, to provide a class of workers
who will accept lower standards of
living. The coolies would be employ
ed to replace Japanese and these, in
turn, would make every effort to rise
above their present economic status,
forcing Americans out of the skilled
trades.
The evidence produced by Presi
dent Wright and Treasurer Chilton
directly contradicts statements made
by sugar planters who have come to
Washington from the Hawaiian is
lands to assist in putting through the
measure to destroy Asiatic exclusion
and open the door to coolie importa
tion and peonage on American soil.
OPTIMISTIC REPORT
BY FEDERAL BOARD
Volume of Retail Trade Compares
With Year Ago With Improved
Outlook.
Washington, Aug. 5. Business re
action in basic lines of industry still
was reflected during July in volume
of business, extent of unemployment,
.and depression of production, said the
monthly review of general economic
conditions issued Monday by the fed
eral reserve board. It was added that
seasonal dullness presented material
alteration in general business condi
tions. J , , -t
Copper and zinc, which were said
to suffer from lack of demand and ex
treme depression' of prices, were men
tioned especially in this connection,
although the board declared there had
been noticeable improvement in pro
duction of cement and structural
steel as a result of the growth in
building operations.
Midsummer retail business, the
board asserted, has been in some re
spects better than usual while the
outlook for a satisfactory autumn
business in a number of lines, such as
knit goods, and cotton textiles, was
declared to be decidedly encouraging,
consumers' demand as reflected in the
volume of retail trade continued as
good or better than at this time last
year.
Continuing prospect of excellent
food crops was declared to be the
most hopeful feature in the general
outlook. Little change in cotton crop
was noted, with about two-thirds of
last year's production expected.
Price changes during the month
have shown no pronounced tenden
cies, the board stated. Greater stabil
ity was manifest in the textile, boot
and shoe industries, although import
ant declines have taken place in other
lines, such as iron and steel and par
ticularly automobiles. The unemploy
ment situation has remained practi
cally unchanged, according to the re
view, with labor authorities report
ing serious depletion of the savings
of men who have been out of work as
well as of unemployment funds of
trade unions.
Desire to see a resumption of full
production, the board said, apparently
has led to operation of some plants on
a non-profit making basis, with wage
scales correspondingly reduced on
employes working on part time.
Money rates have become distinctly
easier the review continued, but
fluctuations in exchange and unstable
foreign financial conditions have pre
vented any material improvement in.
foreign trade.
CLOAK MAKERS WIN.
Philadelphia, July 29. After a two
weeks' strike organized cloak makers
won a complete victory in the shops
of cloak jobbers and sub-manufacturers.
The union has also secured an
agreement with the Cloak Manufac
turers' Association. Former stand
ards are retained and the employers
agree not to send work to shops that
are unfriendly to the union.
FRUIT BUYER SAYS
HIGH RATES WILL
DESTROYJNDUSTRY
Gives Startling Examples of How the
Growers Are Being Bled
White.
Charges that the railroads are
spreading the story that ctmimission
men and brokers are responsible for
high prices in an attempt to deceive
the public and get away with high
freiglU rates were made by Charles
J. brand of Pittsburgh, vice-president
and general manager of the American
Fruit and Vegetable Association, in
testimony given before the joint
commission of the Senate and House
which' is conducting an inquiry into
what is wrong with agricultural con
ditions. Mr. Brand until two years
ago was chief of the Federal market
ing bureau.
"If the present rates are maintain
ed by the railroads for another year
the agriculture, stock-growing and
fruit-raisig industries of this nation
will be absolutely ruined. The freight
charges of the association 1 represent
have been increased from $2,500,000
to $4,000,000 a year on the same
amount of business by the last sched
ule of rates fixed by the Interstate
Commerce Commission."
"Hundreds of millions of dollars of
investment are threatened by prevail
ing rates," said Mr. Brand. "It seems
hard to make the railroads realize it,
bub they are crucifying their own in
dustry along with our other basic in
dustries." Mr. Brand gave some startling il
lustrations of how the farmer is be
ing bled white at one end of the rail
road line and the consumer robbed
at the other. Here are a few sam
ples: $217 a Car for 32 Miles.
A 14-ton carload of cauliflower
from Florin, Cal., to Pittsburgh, net
ted the grower $128.62 after, the rail
roads had been paid $508.61 for haul
ing. For hauling a carload of peaches
from Leesburgh, Va., a distance of
only 32 miles, the railroad charged
$217, which was 50 per cent, of the
total received by the grower.
Several carloads of early cabbage
shipped from Texas to Chicago re
sulted in a loss of $73 per car to the
grower after the railroads had col
lected $414.20 per car for freight, an
increased rate of $125 per car since
August, 190.
Retailers are charging consumers
unwarranted prices, and if continued
it may be necessary for the states to
inaugurate a licensing system that
would regulate the number of retail
ers and sales prices, was the opinion
of the witness.
Says Credit Is Prime Need.
Carl Vrooman, former assistant
secretary of agriculture, told the com
mission that a foreign market to take
the surplus produce of the American
farmer was essential to relieve the
situation.
"Foreign countries want all the
produce we have, but can not buy for
lack of credit," said Mr. Vrooman.
"The United States government is the
only agency which can establish the
necessary credit."
"I do not think the trouble in this
country is back of wealth," Mr. Vroo
man told the commission. "We are
reeking with wealth ;on every hand
we stumble over it. This country has
more wealth than any nation has
ever had in the world. We have a
plethora of wealth; and yet we are
going broke surrounded by wealth.
If we could let some of this wealth go
out we would be like a man whose
blood is too rich and who had paraly
sis, and it would be a jolly good
thing if we could thin our blood a
little.
'"'what we need is a market. Noth
ing will save us except a market for
our surplus products. If we could
send Europe a billion dollars today
we would be better off a billion dol
lars of agricultural products. That
would strengthen us and the money
when recieved in return would be like
so much velvet."
ENGINEERS SHOW
WASTE IiNBUILDING
Vast Sum and Production Could Be
Saved if Bosses Took
Care, Too.
New York, Aug. 5. Building
trades workmen are employed but 190
days a year.
Unemployment causes an annual
loss of $500,000,000.
Accidents cause an annual loss of
$120,000,090.
Duplication of estimates and de
signs and duplication in bidding cause
an annual loss of $1,000,000,000.
Application of safety methods
would save 12,000,000 days' work a
year.
The above are some of the con
clusions reached by the committee on
elimination of waste in industry of
the American engineering council, ap
pointed by Herbert Hoover.
"Haphazard management in plan
ning and controlling work and lack
of standards, which often double the
labor cost, characterizes most con
struction undertakings," says the re
port, which adds that in the past
union regulations produced losses, but
"workmen and contractors are begin
ning to appreciate that reduced output
reacts in tremendous fashion upon
themselves.
"In some construction trades acci
dents involve losses up to 10 per cent,
of the labor costs in addition to the
loss of human lives and energy. The
average loss, computed from insur
ance statistics, is about 2jj per cent,
of labor cost."
The report insists that greater co
operation is "an absolute essential,"
and that this co-operation is impossi
ble "without the removal of causes
of friction and the working out of
plans to this end."
MANY WOMEN WORKING.
Cleveland, Aug. 5. There are 8,
000,000 women in Uiis country en
gaged in gainful occupations, said
Dr. Amelia Henry Reinhardt, of Oak
land, Cal., in an address to the na
tional federation of business and pro
fessional women's clubs.
She said that 50 per cent, of the
women are teachers and 37 per cent,
occupy secretarial positions. Only
1,600 have graduated in law.

THE BOBOLINKS.
"I heard," said Mr. Bobolink, "that
the Phoebe Birds had told their story
of how they like to get acquainted
with people, and have people become
acquainted with them, and so they
sing their name or say their name
over and over again. But we would
like to tell people that we do the
same way. Wouldn't we like to do
that, Mrs. Bobolink?"
"Indeed we would," said Mrs. Bob
olink. "For if anyone does not know
our story it is time that they should
that is, of eourse, if they like to hear
the stones the birds have to tell.
"I most certainly hope they do, for
the birds love to tell their stories and
have people care for them Birds
care for people, and so it is natural
that they like to have people care for
them.
"The only people birds don't care
for are for those who would hurt
them or take the eggs. It would be
too cruel for anyone to ever take our
lovely eggs. What beautiful whitish
brownish eggs these five eggs were !
And what nice little Bobolinks they
became.
"Why, It would be as cruel to do
that as it would be to put a sharp
sword through Mother Bobolink's
heart.
"It would be cruel If people took
little babies out of their sleeping bas
kets or cots and took them off just
to see what , color eyes they had and
how big they were, and a few little
facts like that. '
"It would be so cruel a thing to do:
And people can enjoy birds so much
more by hearing them sing and by
"You Are Watching."
watching them perched on trees and
grasses and fences, or by watching
them fly through the air, showing
their pretty feathers.
"People wouldn't like It if bobo
links or other birds ran off with their
little ones, and so it isn't fair to do
such things to bobolinks or other
birds. It isn't fair at all. It is very,
very cruel. And just because people
are bigger than we are It Is even more
cruel.
"Of course, It would be Impossible
for bobolinks and other bird families
to hurt people. But isn't it even worse
of people to hurt us because they
are strong enough to do so? I think
anyone who steals birds' eggs Is a
bully, that's all, doing something
mean to a little creature more help
less than he or she is.
"But let us not talk of such a sad
subject, though it was I who started
the talk on it. Let us talk of what
you started to speak of how the
Phoebe Birds told their story of how
they liked to get acquainted with
people."
"Yes," Mr. Bobolink said, "the
Phoebe birds told how they said
'Phoe-be, Phoe-be' over and over again
so people would know who they were.
"And I'd like to have people know
that we say 'Bob-o-llnk, Bob-o-link'
over and over again to let them know
who we are.
"We add a few trills to our song
which the Phoebe birds don't bother
about, but just the same we sing our
name over and over again, very clear
ly and distinctly."
"You always guard me so well,"
said Mrs. Bobolink. "You do not let
any harm come to me. And if I am
busy looking for food, though you may
be singing for all you are worth, you
are watching out, too, to see that we
are safe and that no one will harm
us. and you warn rne In plenty of
time.
"Ah, Mr. Bobolink, it is not every
creature who goes marketing and who
does her household tasks who can
have beautiful music to listen to as
she works. But you sing for me,
with your lovely voice, and again and
again you tell me your name Is Bob-o-llnk
! To be sure I know it is. But
to me the name is so sweet that I
cannot hear It too often. No, Mr.
Bobolink, I cannot hear the name of
Bobolink too often."
"That is good," said Mr. Bobolink.
And putting his head on one side he
sang over and over and over again,
"Bob-o-link, Bob-o-link, Bob-o-link!"
New Relativity Theory.
James had frequently heard his
uncle discussing the relativity theory
When his uncle married and Intro
duced his bride to James as his new
aunt he asked the boy laughingly
whether he wasn't proud t have such
a pretty girl for a relative. James
looked up solemnly and said.
"Oh, I know ! She's the new rela
tivity theory you were talking about !'
PRESIDENT HATCH RESIGNS.
New York, July 29. James H.
Hatch, president of the International
Upholsterers' Union, has resigned be
cause ok ill health.
The annual convention of the up
holsterers elected William Kohn of
New- York, president.

"A Princess of Mars
by EDGAR RICE
Copyright, A. C.
CHAPTER X Continued.
ed Dejah Thorls by the hand and, mo
tionhig to Kola to follow, we sped
noiselessly from the chamber and to
the floor above. Unseen we reached a
rear window and with the straps and
leather of my trappings I lowered first
Sola and then Dejah Thoris to the
ground below. Dropping lightly after
them I drew them rapidly .around the
court in the shadows of the buildings,
and thus we returned over the same
course I had so recently followed from
the distant boundary of the city.
We finally came upon my thoats in
the courtyard where I had left them,
and placing the trappings upon them
we hastened through the building to
the avenue beyond. Mounting. Sola
In the Same Deathly Silence I Grasped
Dejah Thoris by the Hand.
upon one beast, and Dejah Thoris be
hind me upon the other, we rode from
the city of Thark through the hills to
the south.
No word was spoken until we had
left the city far behind, but I could
hear the quiet sobbing of Dejah Thoris
as she clung to me with her dear head
resting against my shoulder.
"If we make it, my chieftain, the ,
debt of Helium will be a mighty one;
greater than she can ever pay you;
and should we not make it," she con
tinued, "the debt Is no less, though
Helium will never know, for you have
saved the last of our line from worse
than death."
I did not answer, but Instead
reached to my side and pressed the
little fingers of her I loved, where they
clung to me for support, and then, in
unbroken silence, we sped over the yel
low, moonlit moss; each of us occu
pied with his own thoughts.
Our earlier plans had been so sad'.y
upset that we now found ourselves
without food or drink, and I alone was
armed. We therefore urged our beasts
to a speed that must tell on them sore
ly before we could hope to sight the
ending of the first stage of our journey, j
We rode all night and all the follow
ing day with only a few short rests.
On the second night both we and our
animals were completely fagged, and
so we lay down upon the moss and
slept for some five or six hours, taking
up the journey once more before day
light. All the following day we rode,
and when, late in the .afternoon, we
had sighted no distant trees, the mark
of the great waterways throughout ail
Barsoom, the terrible truth flashed
upon us we were lost.
Evidently we had circled, but which
way It was difficult to say. nor did it
seem possible with the sun to guide
us by day and the moons and stars by
night. At any rate no waterway was
in sight, and the entire party was al
most ready to drop from hunger, thirst
and fatigue. Far ahead of us and a
trifle to the right we could distinguish
the outlines of low mountains. These
we decided to attempt to reach in the
hope that from some ridge we might
discern the missing waterway. Night
fell upon us before we reached our
goal and, almost fainting from weari-
ness and weakness, we lay down and
slept.
I was awakened early In the morn
ing by some huge body pressing close ;
to mine, and opening my eyes with a j
start I beheld my blessed old Woola
snuggling close to me; the faithful (
brute had followed us across that!
trackless waste to share our fate. ,
whatever it might be. Putting my arms
about his neck, I pressed my cheek
close to his, nor am I ashamed that I
did it, nor of the tears that came to
ray eyes as I thought of his love for
me. Shortly after this Dejah Thoris
and Sola awakened, and It was decid
ed that we push on at once in an effort
to gain the hills.
We had gone scarcely a mile when I
noticed that my thoat was com
mencing to stumble and stagger in a
most pitiful manner, although we had
not attempted to force them out of a
walk since about noon of the precede
ing day. Suddenly he lurched wildly
to one side and pitched violently to
the ground. Dejah Thoris and I were
thrown clear of him and fell upon the
soft moss with scarcely a jar; but the
poor beast was in a pitiable condition,
not even being able to rise, although
relieved of our weight. Sola told me
that the coolness of the night, when it
fell, together with the rest, would
doubtless revive him, and so I decided
not to kill him, as was my first inten
tion, as I had thought it cruel to leave
BURROUGHS
McClurg & Company.
him alone there to die of hunger and
thirst. We left the poor fellow to his
fate ant! pushed on with the one thoat
as best we could. Sola and I walked,
making Dejah Thoris , ride, much
against her will. In thfs way we had
progressed to within about a mile of
the hills we were endeavoring to reach
when Dejah Thoris, from her point of
vantage upon the thoat, cried out that
she saw a great party of mounted men
filing down from a pass in the hills
several miles away. Sola and I both
looked in the direction she indicated,
and there, plainly discernible, were
several hundred mounted warriors.
They seemed to be headed in a south
westerly direction, which would take
them away from us.
They doubtless were Thark war
riors who had been sent out to cap
ture us, and we breathed a great sigh
of relief that they were traveling in
the opposite direction. Quickly lifting
Dejah Thoris from the thoat, I com
manded the animal to lie down and we
three did the same, presenting as small
an object as possible for fear of at
tracting the attention of the warriors
toward us.
As what proved to be the last war
rior came into view from the pass, he
halted and, to our consternation, threw
his small but powerful fieldglass to his
eye and scanned the sea bottom in all
directions. As his glass swung toward
us our hearts stopped in our breasts
and I could feel the cold sweat start
from every pore in my body.
Presently it swung full upon us and
stopped. Then he lowered it and
we could see him shout a command to
the warriors who had passed from our
sight behind the ridge. He did not
wait for them to join him, however;
instead he wheeled his thoat and came
tearing madly in our directioin.
There was but one slight chance
and that we must take quickly. Rais
ing my strange Martian rifle to my
shoulder I sighted and touched the
button which controlled the trigger;
there was a sharp explosion as the
missile reached its goal, and the charg
ing chieftain pitched backward from
his flying mount.
Springing to my feet I urged the
thoat to rise, and directed Sola to take
Dejah Thoris with her upon him and
make a mighty effort to reach the hills
before the green warriors were upon
us. I knew that In the ravines and
gullies they might find a temporary
hiding place, and even though they
died of hunger and thirst it -ould be
better so than that they fell Into the
hands of the Tharks. Forcing my -two
revolvers upon them as a slight means
of protection, and, as a last resort, as
an escape for themselves from the hor
rid death which recapture would sure
ly mean, I lifted Dejah Thoris in my
arms and placed her upon the thoat be
hind Sola, who had alreadymounted
at my command.
'Good-hy, my princess," I whispered,
"we may meet in Helium yet. I have
escaped from worse plights than this,"
and I tried to smile as I lied.
"What." she cried, "are you not com
ing with us?"
"How may I, Dejah Thoris? Some
one must hold these fellows off for a
while, and I can better escape them
alone than could the three of us to
gether." She sprang quickly from the thoat
and, throwing her dear arms about my
neck turned to Sola; saying with quiet
dignity: "Fly, Sola! Dejah Thoris re
mains to die with the man she loves."
Those words are engraved upon my
heart. Ah, gladly would I give up ray
life a thousand times could I only hear
them once more; but I could not then
give even a second to the rapture of
her sweet embrace, and pressing my
lips to hers for the first time, I picked
her up bodily and tossed her to her
seat behind Sola again, commanding
the latter In peremptory tones to hold
her there by force, and then, slapping
the thoat upon the flank, I saw them
borne away; Dejah Thoris struggling
to the last to free herself from Sola's
grasp.
Turning, I beheld the green war
riors mounting the ridge and looking
for their chieftain. In a moment they
saw him, and then me; but scarcely
had they discovered me than I com
menced firing, lying flat upon my belly
in the moss. I kept up a continuous
stream of fire until I saw all of the
warriors who had been first to return
from behind the ridge either dead or
scurrying to cover.
My respite was shortlived however,
for soon the entire party, numbering
some thousand men, came charging
into view, racing madly toward nie. I
fired until my rifle was empty and
they were almost upon me, and then
a glance showing me that Dejah Thoris
and Sola had disappeared among the
hills, I sprang up, throwing down my
useless gun, and started away In the
direction opposite to that taken by
Sola and her charge.
If ever Martians had an exhibition
of jumping, it was granted those as
tonished warriors on that day long
years ago, but while It led them away
from Dejah Thoris it did not distract
their attention from endeavoring to
capture me.
They raced wildly after me until,
finally, my foot struck a projecting
piece of quartz, and down I went
sprawling upon the moss. As I looked
up they were upon me, and although I
drew my longsword In an attempt to
sell my life as dearly as possible, it
was soon over. I reeled beneath their
blows which fell upon me in perfect
torrents ; my head swam ; all was
black, and I went down beneath them
to oblivion.
'CHAPTER XI.
Chained in Warhoon.
It must have been several hours be
fore I regained consciousness. I was
lying among a pile of sleeping silks
(Continued next issue.")
English Miners'
Official May Quit
Secretary Severely Criticized for
Conduct of Recent Strike
Failure.
London, England, Aug. 5. It is
hinted that the attack of Frank
Hodges, secretary of the Miners'
rederation, against extremists is the
forerunner for the resignation of that
official. Throughout the recent strike,
which ended so disastrously to the
miners, Hodges was subjected to
much criticism from within the
miners' ranks. During the strike he
offered to resign, but it was not ac
cepted. It is stated that Hodges will
quit rather than be in continual tur
moil over impossible proposals. He
has declared that if the miners' offi
cials were permitted to use their
judgment they would be in better
shape than now.
"l'arrot-like, we were crying 'pool,'
when we should have grasped wages
with both hands," he said.
The proposed "pool" provided that
each ton of coal mined would be
assessed and the fund placed in
charge of a joint committee that
would use it to equalize wages be
tween high cost and low cost mines.
Labor Fully Alert
to Coolie Menace
Unionists All Over Country Join in
Protest Against Letting
Down Bars.
Washington, Aug. 5. Reports to
A. F. of L. headquarters show that
trade unionists in every section of the
country are alert to the menace of
coolie labor, and to the designs of
those who would permit Hawaiian
sugar planters importing this labor
under bond for five years. Trade
unionists realize the effect of this
peonage on Hawaiian workers and
they also realize that with this prin
ciple established it would be but a
short time before it would be ap
plied to the United States on the
ground that it is unfair to ask sugar
planters in this country to compete
with coolie labor. The next step
would be in the fruit industry of the
Pacific coast, followed by railroad
managers and captains of industry for
this cheapest labor in the world.
The views of these employers is
shown when ship owners recently de
nied the request of seamen and ma
rine engineers to agree to give Amer
ican citizens first preference in em
ployment. The United States ship
ping board sustained the ship owners
in their position.
JThe Story of
Our States
By JONATHAN BRACE
X. VIRGINIA
THE EARLY
history of
Virginia re
volves about
the name of
Capt. John
Smith. It was
his indom
itable spirit which enabled the
settlement at Jamestown to sur
vive the privations and difficul
ties that encompassed it during
those first years. There in 1607
was made the first permanent
English colony in America. The
charter given by King James I
to what was known as the Lon
don company, under whose aus
pices Jamestown and the later
Virginia settlements were found
ed, designated that this colony
should extend 200 miles north
and 200 miles south of Old
Point Comfort and stretch west
ward to the sea. Under the reign
of Charles II it was considered
such an important colony that it
was called the fourth dominion
of his empire, that is, England,
Scotland, Ireland and Virginia.
From this Virginia was often
called the Old Dominion. The
name Virginia itself means the
virgin land and was so called
after Queen Elizabeth, the "Vir
gin Queen." The early govern
ment of Virginia is of great in
terest. At first communism was
tried, under which system the
land and all property was owned
in common and all profits were
divided equally. This proved a
disastrous failure. In 1619 the
first real representative govern
ment in the colonies was estab
lished, called the house of bur
gesses, which continued in force
under varying fortunes until Vir
ginia joined the Union and be
came the tenth state in 1788.
Much might be written of the
character of the early Virginian
settlers and their great influence
on American life. From Virginia
has sprung two other states,
Kentucky and West Virginia.
Even so, the state's present area
is 42,627 square miles. Politi
cally the Old Dominion state is
entitled to twelve presidential
electors. This is about the aver
age of the southern states.

OFFICIAL PAPER
EVERETT CENTRAL
LABOR COUNCIL
VOL. XXX.
THE OFFICIAL BULLETIN OF THE
WN. STATE FEDERATION OF LABOR
SPOKANE LABOR PUTS EASTERN WASHINGTON
FAIR IN THE "RED"
♦
What Will Happen at Puyallup?
The Spokane labor movement
placed the Spokane Fair on its offi
cial unfair list many months ago,
following the refusal of the man
agement to employ any union labor
at the prevailing scale. The man
agement announced early this year
tnat the highest scale to be paid
to any mechanics would be $5.0u
per day. The scale paid by the
city of Spokane, and by all con
tracting firms, for all classes of
mechanics, is far in excess of $5.00
per day. All efforts of the Build
ing Trades Council and Central
Labor Council to have the matter
properly adjusted met with failure,
with a result that the Fair was
finally placed on the unfair list.
The spokane labor movement
waged an active campaign against
patronage of the Fair, with a re
sult that on last week Monday,
when the Fair opened, the attend
ance showed only approximately
11,000 as compared to over 29,
--doo on the same day last year. The
attendance at every succeeding da>
was kept proportionately lower with
a' result that a large deficit will
have to be made up by the busi
ness and other interests of the
city of Spokane.
Business and professional men
from every walk of life in the city
of Spokane are openly condemning
the bone-head management that
brought about this condition, and
are openly commending labor for
its stand. Many of those who im
agined labor was dormant and no
longer possessed really effective
VVHAT ABOUT PUYALLUP?
The building trades councils of
the cities of Tacoma and Seattle,
supported by the State Federation
of Labor in convention, have placed
the Puyallup Fair on the official
unfair list of the state labor move
ment. The conditions that brought
about this action on the part of
the labor movement were much more
provocative than those that in
spired like action at Spokane.
$3.50 and $4.00 per day was the
maximum paid for carpenters and
other mechanics on the construction
work of the Puyallup Fair. All
efforts to have union men employ
ed and the prevailing scale paid,
resulted in failure.
The Puyallup Fair has succeeded
in former years due largely to the
general support it has received
from labor and its friends. The la
bor movement of Western Wash
NEW AFFILIATIONS AND RE-AFFILIATIONS WITH
STATE FEDERATION INCREASING
The campaign for new affiliations
and re-affiliations of delincpuent lo
cals is steadily gaining ground. A
total of twelve were received last
week at the State Federation office,
embracing the following unions:
Aberdeen Cooks and Waiters, and
Musicians; Bellingham Barbers; Cen
tralia Mine Workers; Puyallup Car
penters; Seattle Lathers, Bill Pos-
MINERS' INJUNCTION CASE STILL PENDING
The miners' injunction case is
still pending before the Superior
Court at Seattle. As the Bulletin
is being compiled this week the case
is being argued before Judge Aus
tin E. Griffiths of the Superior
Court at Seattle.
Judge Griffiths in February of
this year, in the case of the Mold
ers' Union, versus the Employers'
Association, at Seattle, ruled that
there was no basis in law that jus
tified the employer prohibiting the
Molders' Union from peaceably pre
venting the other workers from tak
ing their places of employment and
threw the entire case out of court.
It is believed a similar decision
will be rendered in the present in
junction case. The Pacific Coast
Coal Co. is represented by their own
attorney, Mr. Stratton, and by Mr.
Halverstad, attorney for the Em
ployers' Association. The miners
are represented by their regular at
torney George R. Rummens. and his
associate, Mr. Griffin. An early
decision is expected, and one fa
vorable to the right of the miners
to peacefully persuade the imported
scabs from taking their places.
The amended Clayton Act. passed
by the 1919 Legislature through the
efforts of the State Federation of

Union Theater
Employees Win
A Big Victory
New York, Sept. 10.—The anti
union shop in the theaters of the
country has been kicked out of the
stage doors and the union workers
have walked back to their old jobs.
The managers got tired of the
anti-union shop principle even be
fore they attempted to put it into
effect as a workable' proposition.
The settlement entered into be
tween the International Alliance of
Theatrical Stage Employes, the
American Federation of Musicians
and the Producing Managers' Asso
ciation guarantees the union shop
and collective bargaining for an
other year in those branches of the
theatrical profession. The settle
ment for the burlesque theaters
puts about ten more theaters under
the banner of the union shop and
gains for the workers an increase
in wages.

CONFERENCE ON
UNEMPLOYMENT
ASKED OF HART
Organized labor forwarded a
communication to the state capital
Saturday urging Gov. Louis F.
Hart to summon a conference on
unemployment.
Under labor's plan all state,
county and city officials who act
in the capacity of employers, to
gether with representatives of pri
vate employing interests and or
ganized labor, would meet to con
sider plans for combatting the sit
uation.
Saturday's call on Governor Hart
follows the adoption by the Cen
tral Labor Council of its strategy
committee report recommending
such action. In the strategy com
mittee's report local unions were
urged to rotate the work of their
crafts in order to give jobs to idle
members. Following is Secretary
James Duncan's letter to Hart:
"As you are aware, to the aver
age citizen the most dreaded spec
tre and the worst menace to happi
ness is the possibility of unem
ployment; while in the very nature
of things we all recognize that if
our people are to enjoy life, liberty
ami the pursuit of happiness they
must, in order to come by these
things honestly, have employment.
"To deprive anyone of opportunity
to work is equivalent to denying that
one the means of life, and to do
so is to commit tin unpardonable sin
for which those in control are re
sponsible, and not nature; hence the
justifiable resentment upon the part
of those who suffer as a conse
quence.
"Last winter it was estimated
that approximately 10,000 people
in Seattle were out of employment,
while in addition many more thou
sands of their dependents suffered
with them.
"The Social Welfare League l re
ports that in July, 1921, it was
called upon to give relief to more
than twice the number given relief
in July, 1920, and prospects for the
coming winter with an estimate of
eight thousand unemployed al
ready in this city, are, to say the
least, disquieting, and what is true
of this locality is equally true of
other parts.
"The Central Labor Council of
Seattle and vicinity, recognizing the
gravity and injustice of this situa
tion and knowing that a solution is
possible, respectfully urges you,
whose function we feel it is, to act
immediately in this matter.
"Acting upon the theory that
employers control the jobs, which
can be increased or decreased ac
cording to their will and the per
iod of time each employe works;
and that labor needs the jobs in
order to secure an honest liveli
hood, we request that at the ear
liest possible date, you call a con
ference of all state, county and city
officials of Washington who act in
the capacity of employers, together
with representatives of private em
ploying interests and of organized
labor for the purpose of formulat
ing plans such as will result in a
division of present available work
and the opening up of new public
and private works that will solve
our unemployment problem, taking
such steps as may be necessary to
guard against an influx from other
states.
"We are convinced of the vital
necessity, the justice and the prac
ticability of such action as suggest
ed and in the interests of all of the
people of this great comonwealth
again earnestly request your favor
able consideration of same."
THE CHANGES IN
RETAIL PRICES
The U. S. Department of Labor,
through the Bureau of Labor Sta
tistics, has completed the compila
tions showing changes in the retail
cost of food in August, in 15 princi
pal cities of the United States.
During the month from July 15
to August 15, 1921, there was an
increase in all of these cities. In
Rochester there was an increase of
8 per cent; in Buffalo, 7 per cent;
in Baltimore and New York, 6 per
cent; in Milwaukee, Newark and
Norfolk, 5 per cent; in Charleston,
S. C, Louisville, Manchester, and
Portland, Me., 4 per cent; in Hous
ton, 3 per cent; in Butte and Dal
las, 2 per cent; and in Minneapolis,
1 per cent.
For the year period August 15,
1920, to August 15, 1921, there was
a decrease of 30 pre cent in Butte;
28 per cent in Louisville; 20 per
cent in Baltimore, Charleston, S. C,
Dallas, Milwaukee and Minneapolis;
25 per cent in Buffalo, Manchester
and Norfolk; 24 per cent in Hous
ton, Newark, Portlank, Me., and
Rochester; and 22 per cent in New
York.
As compared with the average
cost in the year 1913, the retail
cost of food on August 15, 1921,
■showed an increase of 02 per cent
in Manchester; 00 pel- cent in
Buffalo and Milwaukee; 59 per cent
in New York: 58 per cent in Bal
timore and Charleston, S. C; 50
per cent in Minneapolis and New
ark; 47 per cent in Dallas; and
43 per cent in Louisville. Prices
were not obtained from Butte, Hous
ton, Norfolk, Portland, Me., or
Rochester in 1913, hence no com
parison for the 8-year period can
be given for these cities.

STRIKE INS. CO.
IN SCRAP HEAP
Printing Trades 44-Hour Strike
Broke Its Back
The strike insurance and strike
breaking plan of the Employer's
Mutual Insurance Service Company
has hit the rocks.
A commission appointed by the
court reports that the company's
liabilities exceed its assets by about
¥1,000,000 and possibly considerably
more.
Circuit Judge Dobbler has or
dered that a receiver be appointed
for the company unless it proves it
is capable of paying its debts.
State Insurance Commissioner
Keating was a member of the com
mission that investigated the
company under order of the court.
The following recommendation was
included in the commission's report:
"That an injunction be issued
suspending the business of the com
pany and that receivers be appoint
ed to take charge of the estate anil
assets of the company and to ad
minister the same and wind up its
affairs under the direction of this
honorable court and that the' char
ter of said company, as an insur
ance company, be annulled and for
feited."
The commission told the Court
that the company was doing busi
ness in violation of the laws of the
state of Maryland and had not suffi
cient assets to carry on its busi
ness; that it was insolvent and
unable to pay its losses.
The commission indicates that
the 44-hour strike of the printing
trades was the final blow to the
company.
Organized labor has been cease
less in its publicity agitation that
the company is a strikebreaking in
stitution. This position is sustained
by Leroy Lane, assistant to Presi
dent Edward C. Myers of the com
pany, who acknowledged that the
company maintained an employ
ment service to furnish employes
to employers who were having: la
bor troubles.
In acknowledging that organized
labor exposed this company Lane
pleaded that employers who had in
sured should ,waive their claims
rather than permit trade unionists
to point to this latest victory.
"I believe the insured would put
beyond any Question of indemnity,
if necessary, the moral effect of
continued operation of the com
pany", said Lane. "If receivers are
anpointed it will be the biggest
feather in the cap of organized la
bor that has ever happened in this
country."

United States Attorney General

The men and women of labor

Mr. Daugherty says that "the

In addition to having committed

torney general fails to find the
tiuth in history.
The "experience of the past" does
not show that industrial adjustment

der conditions which they find

ized American industry, are the only

Poll Tax Qualifies
A Citizen for Jury
Duty, Court Rules
Bellingham, Sept. 12.—Judge Har
din ruled this morning that a pay
ment of the $5 poll tax makes a
man sufficiently a taxpayer to
qualify him for jury service. It
is the law that before a man or
woman may sit as a juror he or she
must be a taxpayer of the county.
■ Hardin said he had not heard of any
I decision on the subject, but had
' concluded that payment of the poll
tax makes one a taxpayer just the
same as paying tax on persona!
! property or realty.
I The matter came up in the test
ing of A. L. Lorenz, a young farm
er in the Mountain View district,
as to his qualifications to serve as a
juror in the September term be
ginning today. He said he had
never paid any tax but the poll
tax.

the case was docketed, evidently
realizing the exorbitant sum de
! manded as bail to be in violation of
| the Constitution of the Uited
,St ites. reduced the original amount.
But the reduction in ijail will have
little influence in getting the ar
rested men their liberty. The main
purpose served will be that the
smaller amount looks better, in
prim and gives it the appearance
01 "justice tempered with mercy."
The bail must be provided in real
properly "without encumbrance."
Commenting on tho strike out of
which the charges of conspiracy
have been made, the Bakers' Jour
nal says "that when the full his
tory of the crimes committed dur
ing the bakers' strike will be writ
ten it will be found that the re
sponsibility will have to be laid
at the doors of the Chicago Bak
ers Employers' Association. The
Bakers' Union disclaims all respon
sibility." "Confessions" made by
employers' spies in the union are
the basis of the conspiracy charges.
These spies, expelled from the
union, are said to be living at first
ebUM hotels at the expense of the
taxpayers of Cook County.
Philadelphia. Sept. 10.—Automo
bile mechanics and repair men in
the shops of the American Railway
Express Company in this city have
been organized by District 1, Inter
national Association of Machinists.
Similar success in the unionizing of
these mechanics is reported from all
lections of the country. The move
ment is part of the general cam
paign of the International Associa
tion of Machinists, with headquar
ters in Washington, to bring into
the union fold all mechanics en
gaged in the automobile industry.
