The Public Library.
The Public Library of the District of Co
lumbia srrows. But it has many needs
which should be met. The growth and
needs of the library are shown in the an
nual report of the trustees, which was
transmitted to the District Commissioners
yesterday.
In the matter of growth, accession of
hooks, gain in patronage, expansion of util
ity and increase In efficiency of adminis
tration the library has justified the hope of
its founders and other friends. In the year
It has acquired 11,322 new books, bringing
the book strength of the library to 73,045
volumes. The total number of registered
borrowers was 38.779, an Increase of 4,781,
or 12 per cent. The number of books circu
lated during the year was 350,403. an in
crease of 75.308, or 27 per cent. There was
a gain of more than 40 per cent In the
monthly attendance in the reference read
ing room. These statistics tell the story of
the library's growth. The public library j
grows for two reasons. First. It is a good
library; and second, It meets a want In the
national capital which none of the federal
libraries supplies.
One significant feature of the library re
turns is the evident improvement In the
taste of the readers. More and better books
were called for. In the Action circulated
there was a decrease of 12 per cent, and in
the Children's room, where there was a gain
in circulation of 53 per cent. Action declined
1*? per cent.
This is no reflection on fiction In the ap
proved fo'rms. There is fiction that is whole
some heart and brain food and which is es
sential to the man who would be well-read.
There Is classic fiction. In this there was
no decrease Bu-t there was a turning aside
from the books of ephemeral note. Some
of these, of course, are good, but many are
worthless.
There is one need to which the report I
calls emphatic attention. The library should
have a larger collection of books pertaining
to Washington city. The report says:
Endowments or donations to buy certain
classes of books are much needed. For ex- I
ample- the library Is endeavoring to collect
\\ ashingtonlana; 1. e., everything pertaining
to the national capital, and the ten miles
square. Donations not only of books of this
sort, but of money to create a fund for the
purchase of such books, would be heartllv
welcomed."
It Is appropriate that the Public Library
should excel In books on Washington. There
Is no place in the District where books of
this character would do more good. No
doubt many precious tomes treasured up In
the private libraries of the city will event
ually find their way to the public library.
This library is the place to which students
of Washington's story should naturally
turn. It will give a distinctive Washington
atmosphere to the library and will draw to
gether those persons interested In the incep
tion, progress, future and fortunes of the
Capital city.
Among other needs of the library are an (
Increase in the force of assistants, an an
nual appropriation of *15,000 for the pur
chase of books, and the acquisition of mis
cellaneous duplicates from the departmen
tal libraries and the Library of Congress.
The New York Mayoralty Race.
If the nomination of Mr. Hearst for mayor
of New York by the Municipal Ownership
League shall be followed by the nomination
of some attractive high-class man by the
republicans the situation in the metropo
lis will become interesting in the extreme.
The first play, naturally and properly,
was for fusion. Tammany, with Mr. Mc
Clellan as Its candidate, would. It was seen,
be very strong In the race. A pull alto
gether. therefore, by the opponents of Tam
many was wisely recommended. But the
different elements that made up the oppo
sition would not mix, and fusion failed.
Now the question is. can Tammany be
defeated by other means in Its aims for this
year. or. if not for this. In Its alms for
next year? Mr. Hearst, it is to be assumed,
will make a vigorous campaign, and should
draw hla support largely if not entire!*- from
the democracy, while a high-class republi
can candidate should poll the full strength
of that party. Can Mr. Hearst so divide the
democratic vote as to enable the republicans
to win?
If this is not possible, is it possible for the
democratic majority 1n the mayoralty race I
to be kept down to that figure which would
cut the mayor's comb in the gubernatorial
speculation? It Is very clear that a rousing
majority for Mr. McClellan for mayor will
niean his nomination for governor, whereas
a cl03e call for mayor next month might
put him entirely out of the gubernatorial
running. I'p-state democrats might put
their veto on a leadership for state purposes
which had had to beg its way through in a
local content.
But Tammany will endeavor to meet Mr.
Hearst on his own ground. It Intends to
bid against him for municipal ownership
votes. Senator Murphy Is cultivating an
alarm on the subject of corporation selfish
ness and greed that?on paper, at leasv
promises to rival Mr. Hearst's own. He will
pledge Tammany in its platform to do all
that Mr. Hearst wants in that line, and lo
do it with neatness and dispatch. And iiis
cart-tail orators, going up and down tiio
town, will enlarge upon the text in their
most flamboyant and irresponsible fashion.
Keep your eyes on Gotham. It is a great
own to begin with, and Its politics, always
intricate, were never more intricate than
John D. Rockefeller generally manages to
have his own way. He Is now engaged in
giving away millions of dollars without a
word of protest from Dr. Gladden.
Young Mr. Hyde has had his troubles, but
he has at least been lucky, so far, in not
being called to the witness stand.
Coolie Labor in South Africa.
The letter from Pretoria published in
Tuesday's Star, treating In particular of
Chinese crime In the Transvaal and in gen
eral of the coolie system In South Africa,
presents facts which are of interest in the
current discussion of the question of
smoothing the way for the introduction of
coolie labor In the United States.
The Star's correspondent says that the
coolie system of working the Rand mines
haa failed. It was proclaimed that the in
auguration of this system would usher In an
era of prosperity in South Africa such as
had never been witnessed before. "What
ever It may have done to fatten the banking
balances of the mining magnates,'* says
The Star's correspondent, "It has done
nothing to improve the economic condi
tions of the Transvaal. Over 44,000 coolies
iiava been Imported. Their dissatisfaction
with the system may be Inferred from the
fact that despite the vigor displayed tn
hunting them up and bringing them back
to the mines, there are now about a thou
sand of them at large. Regarding themselves
as the victims of white tyranny and In
justice, marauding bands of them do not
scruple to rob and murder white people
when they think they can do so with Im
punity. In the compounds themselves .riots
are of frequent occurrence. Tire coolies are
far more troublesome than ever were the
Kaffirs whose labor they have replaced."
"The Leader" of Johannesburg says that
there are 12.000 British workmen out of em
ployment within a twelve-mile radius of the
Johannesburg market square and that suoh
widespread misery and destitution were
never known before the war. This condition
has aroused a fierce feeling of hostility to
the British government, even :\mong peo
ple of British birth, who feel that the gov
ernment has betrayed the country Into the
hands of capitalists, who are blind to all
Interests but those of their own pockets.
The South Africa coolie problem Is due to
the avarice, the narrowness of understand
ing and the lack of patriotism of the mining
capitalists. It shows for one thing that a
man may have money without brains. We
have men In this country of no bigger cali
ber than these South African mine barons,
but let us hope, not many. Mining reports
published in London show that since the In
troduction of coolie labor the operating ex
penses of the South African mines have In
creased out of ratio to Increase In produc
tion. It costs more to mine a ton of ore
with the cheap. Inefficient slave labor
brought from Asia than It did with the na
tive black labor. Some labor may be bought
eo cheap that It Is not worth the price.
Often the cheapest labor is the most costly
l>ecau%p it Is the least productive. Labor
that la low In price and efficiency is never
cheap in those industries where machinery
may be employed as In mining. The highest
farm wages In the world are paid In North
America and yet North American com and
wheat can be sold as cheaply and perhaps
more cheaply than any other. Very cheap
labor may be used on cotton and tea, be
cause no effective picking machine has been
devised.
Outside of the failure of coolie labor In
South Africa to effect the economies ex
pected of It, It has demoralized the whole
industrial situation there, and thrown out
of employment the men who buy the goods,
Pa> the taxes and support the government.
President McCall and 1904.
?' r. Watterson sees the matter in this
llgfct:
,.?lr; John A. McCall Is a democrat, as
?-1 ' says he Is. he-gave the Mc
ey campaign fund of 1MMJ and 11)00 the
money because he was afraid of Mr. Bryan
and the free silver propaganda. Many
democrats shared his apprehens:on But
there was nothing to frighten Mr. McCall
away from Judgo Parker In 11*04. If he
gave money in that year, and Mr. Perkins
sion " mUSt haVe tM>en on compul
On the contrary, m:?y we not assume that
Mr. McCall felt greater apprehension In
1!*M than in either of the other years? Un
like Mr. Watterson, he was right on the
scene where the Parker g-ime was being
worked. He had noted the performance at
St. Louis, and after the campaign opened
he could almost see from his office window
the place where Thomas P. Ryan, August
Belmont and WHliam F. Sheehan. all cor
porationlsts and In Intimate touch with
trusts and corporations, were busy with
their coalition with Mr. Bryan and his
anti-plutocrats and trus; busters ,of the
south and west. As a New York business
j man. with no political connections, he
could easily appraise the movement and
see that Its success would mean a terrible
and disastrous experience for the country.
Could Judge Parker have named a Sec re
I tary of the Treasury who woUId have been
satisfactory to both factions of his party?
August Belmont was maneuvering for con
sideration, but imagine the effect of his ap
pointment on Mr. Bryan and his friends!
Imagine also the effect on August Belmont
and his friends of the appointment to that
post of any man acceptable to Mr. Bryan
and his friends! And how about the office
of Attorney General? Would Mr. Sheehan,
the attorney of trusts, have consented to
the filling of that office with a man who
would accept his commission as Instruction
to go after the trusts in court? And would
the appointment of any trust lawyer have
faHed to arouse in Mr. Bryan the very
deepest disgust and resentment?
The Bryan campaigns were full of w:nd
and full of menace, but the men conducting
them were sincere. Had either succeeded
the opposition would still have known
where to take hold. But the Parker cam
paign, in Its essence, was bunco, and sue-""
cess would have thrown not only the coun
try Into disorder, but the two parties, and
particularly the democratic party, into such
disorder as had never before been known in
our politics.
As president of the New York Lire In
surance Company, Mr. McCall, using the
company's money, had no right to con
tribute, and should not have contributed, a
red cent to either of the three campaigns in
question. But the Parker campaign had
everything In It to warrant the uneasiness
that It gave him.
Hyde.
James Hazen Hyde should come forward
and tell his story without reservations or
conditions. Let him come, not in a spirit
of bravado, or revenge, nor yet In one of
begging and humility, but as a man who
possesses much information in which the
public Is interested and from which It may
profit. Opinion Is divided about this man.
On the one hand he figures as a weakling,
whose head has been turned by wealth, and
whose capers, social and otherwise, have
been Influenced by older and shrewder men.
On the other hand he figures as a chap who
kiiows the way around, and has played the
E?me simply as he saw others playing It.
Being In New York he did as the New
Yorkers were doing. This much we know?
ho Is free, white and twenty-one?about
thirty, to be exact?and the public wai.ts to
hear from him. Make way for Mr. Hyde.
Representative Dalzell says the treasury
deficit is showing a tendency to disappear.
Mr. Dalzell should be congratulated on his
success in conservative expression.
Mrs. Cassle Chadwlck has evidently man
aged to saved enough out of the wreck to
pay for protracted litigation.
I'nless the czar Is careful Wltte will be
the biggest man In all Russia.
Government-Owned Railroads.
Government control of transportation fa
cilities is a rosy theory and arguments may
b? made in support of It. That It Is a popu
lar theory with a part of the American peo
ple Is not to be denied. That It Is gaining
adherents Is possible. But all facts con
nected with government-run railroads do
not tend to prove the correctness of the
11 eory.
I' or example there Is the Intercolonial
railway of Canada. Though It traverses a
magnificent and well-developed territory
and carries a heavy traffic. It Is being op
erated at a loss. While aU the other rail
way systems in the Dominion show In
creased gross and net earnings for the
year, the Intercolonial, owned by the gov
ernment, shows a deficit of |2.<XKX000 on the
year's business. Its management lacks the
flexibility of the management of the other
roads. It cannot cut expenses when and
where the exigency occurs. To reduce
wages would cause & storm to break which
would wash out the track and carry away
the bridges. To Increase rates would also
bring down the wrath of the electorate.
The following comment on the condition
of the road cornea from a Canadian source:
"The road is generally admitted to be a
good one and it has a capital country to
serve, but political control coats the people
two prices for what they get. Not only has
the railway been made a home for decayed
o- tired politicians?a sort of hospital for
Incurable friends of succeeding govern
ments?but for political purposes rates have
often been fixed absurdly low. Through in
competency at political headquarters,through
the loading upon It for the benefit of polit
ical workers of unprofitable branch lines,
through the interference of partisans with
its operations, through overloading it with
employes and compelling it to run trains
that are not justified by the business done,
the road has been brought to a position
where the more business It gets the greater
Is the loss in operating It."
If the enterprising New York youth who
wanted to show how easy it Is to rrtb a bank
had only confided his Intentions to some one
beforehand his philanthropy would mm
more plausible.
The Norfolk judge who required a quar
relsome husband to kiss his wife twice a
day was either a Solon or a Dogberry.
Time alone will tell.
The next President's message will b? more
than usually interesting, despite the scant
probability of Its figuring as a campaign
document.
Judge Parker will at least have no cause
to complain of leaving public life poorer j
than when he entered It.
After Grover Cleveland gets through dls
cuslng woman's suffrage he may perhaps
be persuaded to say a few words on dress
reform.
Dr. Osier has evidently changed h!a mlncL
No one hears of his asking anybody to pass

There are fears that Pastor Wagner will
grow so popular and prosperous that the

Pat Crowe must realize that he Is not

Mr. Bowen is one of the people who per

That the backbone of summer is broken.

able work in American literature?" said the

?My life insurance company's prospectus,"

Hope.

ball?"
"Yes." answered Farmer Corntossel. "If
he ever gets Into the habit of workin' that
hard, mebbe we'll make the farm pay yet.'"
A. Picnic.
I wisht I was a candidate,
With nothin' much to do
Except to travel 'round the state.
An? make a speech or two;
To have my cash in fives an' tens.
All ready to command,
To call em feller citizens
An' shake 'em by the hand.
An' when the votiil' day was here.
With time an' money spent
I wouldn't very muchly keer
Which way the 'lection went.
The office holder's task Is great
The way to have the fun
Is to remain a candidate
An' jes' keep on the run.
Ten Millions.
From the New York Times.
To the mind accustomed only to ordinary
transactions the payment of ten millions
or dollars in cash is an impressive fact In
Itself, and it is not very many years since
such a payment by an Individual In the
ccurse of business would have aroused a
gcod deal of interest and some wonderment.
But when on Monday the general educa
tion board received that sum from Mr.
Rockefeller in checks?a half dozen of a
million each, and a couple of two millions
each?the matter attracted no especial at
tention, none at all as to the magnitude
of the payment, and not much as to its
object. On the part of the giver there were
evidences also of the commonplace nature
of the incident. The checks were signed
bv his attorneys, as If they conveyed sums
too small and too frequently called for for
the principal to deal with directly. If they
had been warehouse receipts or bills of
lading they would not more completely have
followed the beaten path. The affair, as
one reflects on It, seems saturated with the
flavor of Immense wealth. Poor Balzac,
fleeing from his creditors, and goading his
fancy to the conception of boundless for
tune, never hit upon a set of fictitious facts
so magnificent in their suggestlveness, and
tcday the record of them passes as an Item
of current news, a contribution to the mere
"falts divers" of one big city.
Tuberculosis Congress.
Fro a* the New York Herald.
The International tuberculosis congress
now being held in Paris Is doing excellent
work, the influence of which must be Celt
throughout the entire civilized world. It
is a peace congress In its largest sense, as
It gathers together the experts of all na
tions to decide what is best for humanity
in dealing with a common enemy that de
stroys more than bullet or sword. The
I glory of science Is to save life, and pre
ventive medicine especially claims the
highest of all considerations. The real
good that Is to be accomplished by such
conferences Is the wide publicity given to
their discussions and the consequent
I benefit of the recommendations made.
When It is stated, for instance, that alco
holism, overwork and overcrowding arj
the main predisposing causes of tubercu
losis we can be becomingly impressed
with the vast importance of the purely
sociological problems that must engage
the attention of philanthropists and sani
tary workers the world over. The fact,
although not a new one, is emphasized Iri
a way that gives it a new Importance.
So, also, the necessity for healthy dwell
ings. good food, cleanliness, fresh air and
abundance of sunshine Is again enforced
in a way that cannot be misunderstood.
The Qospel of the Links.
From London Punch.
The bishop of Manchester declares that If
the people will not come to the church the
church must go to the people and follow
them to their week-end resorts. It is, we
hear, proposed to make a start by supply
ing golf links with missionaries, who will
sing a short hymn after each drive. In the
event of a foozle the hymn will be sung
with extraordinary vigor in order to drown
any lay remarks that may be made.
Would Have No Opposition.
From the Syracuse Herald. ~
Mr. Rockefeller insists that people shall
"turn their thoughts to higher things than
mere money getting." Wouldn't he have a
snap then!

Editor Watteraon has deni?l that he Is
an expert in the mint julep lne, and he
disclaims the title of colonel. He continues,
however, to let the star-eyed goddesa alt
on his doorstep.

^^^^^SrjPOSr-OB77CB’, COLOMBO
HE USED to appear at the bung
alow at nine o’clock each
morning, his cocoanut-oileil
skin shining like burnished
metal, eyes aglow with readi
ness, hair done up behind like a wom
an’s. He was a young coolie, lithe
and strong. “Master ready?” “Yes,
Jack.” “Sir, no. John. ‘Jack’ all same
‘Yakka’ (imp).” “Yes, John.” “Sir,
thank you. Master very good mas
ter.”
Out we go on the long white road be
tween sea and jungle, the flexible
spine of my^human steed bending with
each giant stride, and a myriad silver
ed specks of damp starting out on his
dusky skin. The stately palms are still
as hothouse vines, the sea is a sheet
of glass, sun-emblazoned, a menace to
the eye. I open out my big white um
brella, and my cooKe at once adjusts
his swinging step to the changed bal
ance of the little man cart. Others
rickshas, their hoods up, are rolling
on ahead; we come up in their rear,
and other naked-footed runners pad
up behind us and join the human
stream. John’s back is soon a score
of running rills; but except sometimes
to lift a hand from his shafts to wipe
his dripping face with the fringe of
his head cloth, he does not heed.
Sudderwy the leader of the long pro
cession pulls up to brush his strag
gling hair from his eyes, and we ar«
in danger of a series of collisions
But the alert following swerves rapid
ly, and m a second we are a newly led
troop, and the delayed rickasha man,
unless he make special efforts, which
may provoke rivafrous sprinting, must
hang on to our rear.
Through Crowded Streets.
Now we come to the Pettah, and my
oolie breaks away from the main body
and darts into that strange human and
animal medley wThich throngs the na
tive business quarter. ‘Mahata! Ma
hata!” (Master! master!) he cries,
as if to say, “Give place there!” “Ma
hata! Mahata!” answering voices cry,
and some of the lower caste coolies,
almost naked, scurry from before us,
endangering the baskets of freshly
gathered hill-grown tea which they bal
ance on their heads. But the bullock
drivers are of a different tempera
ment. Their guttural cries as they sit
on the front of their tented carts and
poke with naked foot and little stica
and twist the tails of their giant horn
ed, lumbering beasts, almost drown
my coolie's voice. “Hyak! Hyak!” they
rumble loudly; but the wayward bul
locks scarcely respond, and from time
to time the street is completely block
ed. Portly native merchants, tall Af
ghan money lenders and sellers of
betelnut . and other Indian spices
thread their way through the crowd,
and sometimes my vehicle’s shafts
are seized by an insignificant Sinhal
ese policeman, and John is forcibly
shouldered through a widening open
ing. Presently he is prancing un
daunted again through the outskirts of
the locality and into the town, till,
with his breast heaving like the sides
of a blown animal, he sets me down
at my office doorway.
Indefatigable John! He could run
for an hour beneath a zenith sun with
out changing his stride. Sometimes
during the ascent of a hill I might teii
him not to hurry. Always he replies:
“Sir, yes. I liking quickly run.” “Every
day quick, soon die,” I would answer,
for *it is said that the average Indian
ricksha coolie either runs himself to
death or retires in ten years. “Sir,
no; running quickly, making plenty
money; byemby buying one, two, three
cocoanut tree, marry one girl.” “So?”
“Yes sir.” “You thinking you strong,
all same bullock?” I asked. “Master]
no; bullock no good, he lazy.” “Bftt
only bad white man making coolie run
too fast.” “Yes sir. Plenty bad white
man, sir.” And he would spring alert
ly ahead and set me dowrn at my des
tination, and, salaaming, retire to th*
nearest municipal ricksha stand ot
shady spot to rest and chew betelnut,
curling himself up on his shafts un
til some passing pedestrian called him
or I might be ready for him again.
Asks for Holiday.
One evening, several days after I
had freed him for two successive
mornings from his obligation of tak
ing me to business, without deducting
anything from his week's salary, so
that he could journey to a distant
shrine and make his annual salaams
to the Buddha, he asked for a further
holiday to attend a Mohammetan fes
tival. “But you are a Buddhist,” I
said. “Sir, please; Mahomet also very
big Sinhalee feller, sir.” “But you no
give him salaam?” “No, sir. Other
man give. I take him so can give. He
one very big doctor, sir.” John open
ed wide his arms to indicate a man of
immense proportions. “Other coolies
afraid, if taking, he breaking ricksha,
also making coolie very tired. He ask
ing: ‘John* I come your ricksha?’ I
telling: ‘If give one present—medicine
—when I sick in a hurry,’ He say,
Yes.’ ”
“All right, you may go,” I said, and
he trotted away.
When, two days later, he arrived as
usual, I had a headache, my eyes
swam, my limbs felt as if on the rack.
I told Jim, my bungalow boy, to tell
him I had a fever and he could take
still another holiday. Presently Jim
came into the room and stood by my
bed. “Sir, that John coolie telling very
sorry. He staying in compound all
morning; will throw' stone at crow in
tree top.” “All right.” Jim departed
to announce my approval, and a mo
ment later there wras the upproarious
sound of a thousand crows rising in
flight from the palm belt round the
compound; then a shout of laughter,
which died away and left the sultry
air noiseless and still; and within the

a carrion bird.
“That John coolie kill one crow,
sir.”
“Bury it,” I said wearily, “deep,
deep, away from the ants and snakes.”
“Yes, sir.” The boy paused a mo
ment, then:
“That John coolio liking bring one
big '.inhalee doctor. His friend, sir.
He give master very good medicine, no
asking money.” »
“No, no,” I said, “bring me more qui
nine.”
One on A. F. Potts.
Alfred F. Potts, who has just re
turned from California, enjoys telling
the following story on himself: “One
day I chopped into an architect’s of
fice in San Diego to inquire about a
building matter. The architect was
reported out for a few minutes, but a
very charming young woman who was
his assistant engaged me in conversa
tion while I waited. She was very
vivacious and attractive. Finally she
turned on me with a pleasant smile
and said:
“ ‘Mr. Potts, aren’t you going * to
make San Diego your home?’
“ ‘I’m afraid not,’ I replied. 1 have
so many ties and Interests in Indian
apolis, you know.’
“ ‘Oh, you ought to break away
while you can,’ she said.. ‘San Diego
is the most wonderful place in the
world for old people.’ ”—Indianapolis
News.
Plague of Cancer In Finland.
A recent report from Finland shows
that eight times as many men have
cancer on the lip as women. Cancer
of the stomach is about as common in
women as in men. The cancer com
mission was appointed by the Finnish
Medical society and made a study of
the cancers in the hands of 313 phy
sicians. It is pointed out that cancers
are less common among the upper
tilass than in the lower class of Fin
land.
I
MEASURE FEET CORRECTLY
Device, Invented by Massachusetts
Man, Equipped with Tape, Fits
Closely Around Foot.
Custom-made shoes arep likely to be
more comfortable through the inven
tion of a Massachusetts man. Where
formerly the cordwainer was obliged
to rely largely on his own judgment
* _ __ •. ~ t_ •
and on such measurements as he
could obtain with a right-angle rule
and a tape measure, he now has a
device by which the correct measure
ments of all important parts of the
n 4- nnn V n In n frttIT min.
utes. There Is a heel-piece and 6ole
of soft leather, the former running
up well above the ankle. Small tape
measures run through slots in both
pieces of leather and when the foot
is adjusted in the device the tapes
are drawn over and aroiind it and the
measurements noted. The advantage
of this contrivance is that instead of
each measurement being .taken sepa
rately, all are taken in proper rela
tion to each other and when the foot
Is in the natural position it should
occupy in the shoe. The result should
be that the shoe is more comfortable.
IS A PUZZLE TO SCIENTISTS
Theory of Atomic Disintegration of
Radium Suggests Large Number
of Unanswered Problems.
“The greatest of our scientists know
/ittle about radium,” remarked Prof.
H. D. Blackie. of the University of
California. “The theory of its atomic
disintegration has suggested a large
number of problems, some of which re
main unanswered. For instance, there
is a mystery concerning the nature of
the ultimate product or products of
the atoms of the two primary elements
of radium—uranium and thorium.
There is some ground, but no positive
proof that lead is the ultimate product
of one, but as to thorium there is no
solution.
“Even the origin of radium Is un
known. We know that it is an ele
ment of intense activity and of great
rapidjty of disintegration, resembling
its short-lived and active constituents.
The period of the average life of
radium is not accurately known, but
it can not be more than a few thou
sand years, some say 2,500 years. Thus
in a few centuries all the radium' in
existence today will be gone. What
there was of it in the world, say at the
time of the building of the Pyramids,
must have largely vanished by this
time through disintegration.”
Human Skull Elastic.
Fortunately the human skull, al
though composed of bones, is elastic—
much more so than one would think.
The average male adult skull, in fact,
is so elastic that it may be com
I pressed lateraly in diameter by a blow
or pressure applied at the center of
area at right angles to the surface at
that point by 1% centimeters, or about
six-tenths of an inch, recovering its
original diameter and form without

Diseases Cured by Cold Air.
Diseases cured or improved by cold
air are specified in American Medicine
as malaria, tuberculosis, digestive dis
turbances, yellow fever, tetanus and
organic troubles affecting the blood
pressure.
Face Immersed In Water.
To enable a person to keep his face
immersed in water for several min
utes, as for facial massage, a Wash
ington man has invented a clip to
close the nostrils and a rubber tube to
admit air through the mouth.
Test for Smokeless Powder.
Ultra-violet rays from a quartz mer
lury vapor lamp have been found to
accelerate the spontaneous decomposi
tion of smokeless powders, and are
purposed as a test of the stability of
such powders.
Many Languages Taught.
French, German and English are
taught in the schools of Holland, and
pupils are required to pass an exam
ination in these languages before grad
uation.
Highest Scientific Station.
The highest scientific station in the
world is on the summit of Mount
Misti, an extinct volcano in southern
Peru. It is 19,000 feet above sea
level.
MAKE STUDY OF ATMOSPHERE
Kites and Balloons Used to Lift Self
Recording Instruments to Obtain
Air Conditions.
M. A. Lawrence Rouch, at the Blue
till observatory, near Boston, in 1894,
was the first to employ kites, attached
to steel wires, to lift self-recording in
struments and so obtain records of the
various conditions in the atmosphere.
Since that year they have been used
extensively in this country, in Europe,
and from the decks of steamships, fre
quently ascending three and four miles
into the air, writes Paul Foster in the
American Review of Reviews. About
the same time the small ballons-sondes,
or sounding balloons, were perfected
in France, and began to be employed to
carry instruments far higher, some
reaching the extraordinary height of
15 miles.
These discoveries and improved
methods of research led to the organ
ization of an international commission
for scientific aeronautics, under the
a> spice's of which measurements are
made simultaneously at an increasing
number of stations throughout the
world. Started as a private enter
prise, the work is now an international
undertaking, and balloons and kites
have been employed monthly for some
years at many stations in Europe, and
within very recent years at one or two
stations in the United States.

move about 50 feet each year. _

Men of science have not yet decided
whether microbes are plants or ani

English mortality figures Indicate
that farmers are the healthiest class

For drying stockings a New Yorker
has patented a wire frame to stretch

Asbestos recently was discovered in
Sardinia in quantities that are ex
pected to make mining profitable.
The amount of starlight reaching
the earth is equal to that of three

More than 1,100 women students are
enrolled at Berlin university this year,
of whom 60 are from the United
States.
Because it has more phosphoric
acid In easily digested shape than any
other fruit, the apple is one of the
best brain foods.
The queen of Bulgaria has a medical
degree, and the young queen of Bel
gium is said to be the only oculist of

An attachment for a gas Jet that
will cook almost anything as well as

^ ( UR
‘ The Fruits of Creeds
'- 1
By HENRY F. COPE f
psspsHg; HAT which is true of mea
is true also of their creeds
wPUnM in at Iea8t one r€8P«ct—
vlMisiy they must be known by
their fruits, by the effects
^" which they produce. The
final test of a creed is not whether it
remains in precisely the form in which
It was held by those who claimed for
it a special revelation, but whether it
is producing worthy results today.
How much better is our day for the
creeds proclaimed to it? What effects
are coming from the defense of the
faith so far as the problems of hu
man misery and need are concerned?
The solemn words have been recited
and their significances expounded for
many a thousand Sundays from many
a pulpit. Is life richer, sweeter, kind
lier? Are men juster? Is the hypo
crite hated and the oppressor cast
down as a result?
The creeds have shed more blood
and cost more human treasure than
any other modern motive or cause.
They must justify themselves in the
life of today. Unless some one shall
show us the .vast human importance of
knowing exactly the intricacies of an
cient and subtle philosophies we may
learn to ignore them all and go
Duttigui witil me uubiut^ba ui
trying to make this a better world to
live in.
It is easy for many in sonorous
tones to recite their faith, with nice
attention to the least syllable and with
a sense of valiant willingness to de
fend its last iota and then to go out
and grind down the face of the poor,
make themselves strong out of the
weakness of others, and turn'the heart
of a devil to the appeals of common
humanity.
Lack the Essential Things.
The trouble with such confessions
of religion is that they have not
enough contact with real living, not
enough concern about real things to
make a man feel their bearing on his
life. They are guilty of limitation to
speculations and analysis of the Deity
instead of indicating conduct, inspir
ing to right relations and noble doing
bf duty in and amongst humanity.
The market and the shop are the
places where the real creed is writ
ten; all the rest, spoken, sung, or
printed, may be but empty words. A
man’s actual beliefs are wrought out
in the stress of life. He may take the
name of the Most High on his lips
with accents of reverence and yet
demonstrate by his dark creed that he
believes only in the worth and power
of the devil.
As we lay our hands on one another,
as we mark our ways through life, as
we leave the impress of ourselves on
others, we are writing our real creeds.
High heaven laughs at our foolish
mouthings of words and watches the
way we go and the works we do, for
these prove the springs of our lives,
these reveal the things we deeply,
truly believe.
If a man says “I believe in God the
Father,” then he will surely live as
one who knows that he is in a great
family, that the universe is a home
and not a jungle; that in the end truth
and right and goodness must prevail;
that the only way of living is that
which brings us all up closer to the
face and glory of that which we call
God.
Make Sure of the Foundation.
It would be a good thing to turn
from looking back over our creeds to
make sure of their foundations—to
take a good, long, clear look to make
sure of their fruitage. Have they any
effect on my life? If they have not
they are drying up the very heart
within me every time I utter empty
words.
Ask, if there is any good in me, to
what creed is it due? To that which
I repeat in the name of the religion
or to that which I am working out
through life’s lessons? What are the
things I cherish most? What is the
purpose of all my striving? Where
lie the deep springs of conduct? To
answer such questions is to uncover
our dominant beliefs.
The creed to cherish is that which
gives strength for worthy living; that
which impels to the realization of our
best at any cost, that which bears the
fruits of godlike kindness, love, and
hope for all our fellows; that which
best helps us to make true, present,
and actual the dreams and passions
that lay behind the ancient ^verds of
faith and bifing to pass the good of alT
our great family here.
Moral Satisfaction.
Nothing in this world is any good to
us only as it supplies a need in our
lives—it must satisfy our physical hun
ger, our social desire, our intellectual
curiosity, our spiritual longings or we
relegate it to the scrap heap. To the
extent that anything thus feeds us and
fills a felt want in our lives is it of
value to us. This is where the preach
ing of the Gospel and Heaven comes
in. It feeds the moral sentiment and
supplies the spiritual longings of our
nature. Wealth, fame and perishable
things are of no value to the soul be
cause an eternal spirit can only be
fed on eternally enduring entities.
The man who has real faith in God
will have plenty of other good things.
’
Real Utopia at Capital’s Front Door
I
WASHINGTON.—It has been claimed
that one can find about anything
desired In Washington, from armadil
los to zithers, but ivhat resident of
this fair city ever suspected that there
is, almost in its very midst, a peace
ful farming community, the residents
of which have perfectly fresh eggs for
breakfast every day in the week with
never a care whether the market price
is 50 cents a dozen or not?
And who ever thought, that within
the shadow of the dome of the capitol,
there is a community of 2,000 souls, to
which even the omnipresent moving
picture theater has never percolated?
Who would believe that in a locality
not far removed from Washington, and
peopled by Washingtonians, the ro
mantic straw ride still exists, and the
historic town hall is the center of so
cial activity? And that the quilting
bee is still an accepted method of
passing the long winter evenings?
Where is the Utopia, where taffy pulls
and lantern parties are still an accept
ed means of entertainment, is asked.
Well, It’s easy to get there. Just
get on a Columbia line car marked
“District line,” and ask the conductor
to let you off at Sixty-flrst street
northeast Then follow your nose, ac
cording to the directions.
The first thing you know you are
right in the heart of Utopia, town hall
and all. It is Capitol Heights, Md.
By all rights it should be a part of the
District, for it is populated by Wash
ingtonians, and Its citizens for the
most part have their business in this
city.
They have real chickens in Cap
itol Heights, hens that lay eggs, and
roosters that crow like all get out at
daybreak every morning.
The social life of the community
centers about the town hall, where
dances, plays and fairs are frequently
given. The town boasts a Ladies’ Im
provement League, whose duty it is
to see that the lights are lighted ev
ery night so that their husbands can
find the way home, and a citizens’ as
sociation which accomplishes progres
sive legislation. On moonlight nights
the Ladies’ Improvement association
realizes a big saving In coal oil, be
cause hubby finds his way to his own
doorstep in the light of the moon.
It is a shame to think that the pro
gressive little town will be before long
swallowed up by the spreading city.
Mrs. Edward B. McLean’s $50,000 Fete
A FUSILLADE of rapidly exploding
dynamite mines and the sudden
transformation of a miniature battle
field set with platoons of manikin sol
diers into “a field of carnage,” with a
fully equipped corps of Red Cross
nurses rushing to do service among the
“wounded” was the crowning feature
In a series of surprises which made
the $60,000 fete Mr. and Mrs. Edward
Beale McLean gave the other night
one of the most astonishing and spec
tacular affairs ever given in Washing
ton. Small fortresses dotted the gar
dens, and cavalry and cannon added
to the realism of the martial picture.
Red Cross ambulances and Red
Cross nurses arranged on wires
charged with electricity whirled rap
idly to the scenes of the several ex
plosions, and within twenty minntes
after the firing of the first mine the
Italian gardens set with fifty bat
talions of soldiers in b^tle array look
ed like a Red Cross hospital camp in
the full tide of a bloody fray.
The occasion marked the climax of
& week’s entertaining planned in hon
or of the delegates to the interna
tional Red Cross conference.
Eight hundred guests, including not
ables from every country in the civi
lized world, attended the reception,
whiA was marked by the elegance of
& court function.
Mrs. Edward Beale McLean, wear*
ing a robe of cloth of gold, embroid
ered in pearls and having among her
jewels the famous Hope diamond, re
ceived with Mme. Baekmeteff, wife of
the Russian ambassador and an aunt
of the host. The gorgeousness of the
floral decorations surpassed anything
Washington has witnessed in a decade,
while In the gold banquet hall stood a
board set with the magnificent gold
service presented to the late Thomas
Walsh by the late Leopold, king of
Belgium. ^
New York caterers served the
guests, and several bands of musicians
distributed throughout the house add
ed to the brilliancy of a splendid fete.
Throughout the entire day electri
cians from New York were at work in
the gardens placing the half hundred
bombs embedded in soft clay, which
“timed” for the demonstration of the
first aid work of the Red Cross hos
pital service, made possible one of the
most thrilling and realistic features
ever devised for the divertisement of
guests.
Midnight Suppers at “Manor House”
ZF the “Manor House” at Randle
1 Highlands retains its new and sud
den popularity Randle Highlands will
come to mean to Washington what
Versailles and St. Cloud were to Paris
in its palmiest days. Some of the gay
est festivities of the season have
taken place there, and quite the most
novel and brilliant so far was that at
which the naval attache of the French
embassy .and Viscountess d'Azy were
hosts the other night. The entertain
ment was a dinner dance to about 300
of the most exclusive circles of the
capital, and the quaint, old-fashioned
manor was transformed for the night
into a veritable Petite Trianon. Ve
netian, Japanese and Chinese lanterns
formed the illumination throughout
the house and on the lawns and piaz
zas. Cabinet officers laid aside their
graver mein, diplomats exchanged the
wiles of state for those of politest
gallantry, and statesmen forgot their
weighty burdens in, order that they
might enjoy to the full “some music,
accompanied by dancing.” as the dash
ing viscountess worded her invitar
tions.
At midnight Supper was served—a
Maryland supper of the sort partaken
in the days when dancers wore knee
breeches, with a plentitude of powder
and beauty patches.
It is chagrin at being left out of
such delightful parties as this, per
chance, that has occasioned the preva
lent custom in certain circles of Wash
ington society of boldly requesting a
bid of the hostess, when it becomes
noised abroad that a festivity of some
sort is pending. In ,the very begin
ning, perhaps, it originated with the
cheerful acquiescence of the mistress of
the White House to the numerous re
quests for an extra card for house
guests to the state functions at the
executive mansion. Of late, however,
it has spread beyond official circles to
a deplorable degree, until nowadays
the hostess who is planning any priv
ate entertainment of any sort must
exercise the utmost tact and dipli
macy to keep her invitation list within
the bounds of her limitations. t
Try to Sins in Senate. Hustled Out
A SENSATIONAL campaign demon
stration was narrowly averted in
the senate the other day. Sergeant
at-Arms Ransdell received warning in
time to prevent a quartet of robust
voices from bursting into song in one
of the public galleries.
The press agent of a music publish
ing house conceived the idea that it
would be great advertising to have
that story telegraphed into every ham
let that the august senators had listen
ed attentively to a campaign song and
then applauded it, resuming their pro
ceedings where the tuneful voices of
his quartet had interrupted them. He
experienced no difficulty in gaining ad
mission with his quartet to one of the
galleries. When the leader took from
his pocket a tuning fork and sounded
it for the key and the singers in sup
pressed tones hummed preparatory
to bursting into song, a senate attache
warned the sergeant-at-arms that an
attack was about to be made on the
dignity of the senate.
Capitol policemen and detectives
hastened to the gallery. A confederate
of the press agent, suspecting some
thing was about to happen, gave his
associates a signal from a doorway
and the singers quickly withdrew from
the gallery. Employes of the sergeant
at-arms escorted the singers oat of the
capltol grounds.
Carrie Nation attempted to address
the senate from one of the galleries
several years ago^ but since then
there has been no encroachment on
the rules of the senate, which pro
hibit demonstrations of any kind In
the galleries.

FROM A CHINESE STANDPOINT.
An Oriental View of Exclusion and
the Treaties.
Chinese Daily Free Press, San Fran
cisco.—The people of California are
again aroused by the sand-lot slogan,
and again “The Chinese must go.”
This time, however, the warcry is
sounded by the newspapers and the
better class of demagogues of the
stump and platform. The wilted
laurels have been torn from the
wrinkled brow of the sand-lot agitat
or, and now adorn the heads of editors
who fancy they think, orators who
chatter but do not think, and politi
cians whose pockets think for them.
During the last week quite a stir has
been created by the holding of public
meetings and by the untrue state
ments and drivelling arguments of
the newspapers. The spasm is or
and for a while temperate and staid
citizens —those whose minds are not
warped by the greed of praise or mon
ey—must wait until the excited public
judgment shall resume its normal. In
the meantime, the Chinese Free Press
will do what it may to rescue the
truth from the deluge of falsehood
and vituperation which now sub
merges it.
There is nothing intricate or com
plicated in the position of the Chinese
people upon the treaties or the ex
clusion law. They are of American
construction, and the Chinese are
willing to abide by and obey all the
provisions thereof. They demand
that the compliance shall be mutual,
and that individual injustice shall not
be inflicted upon them. They offer
no objection to the strictest exclusion
of that class of their countrymen
which might conflict with the mater
ial interests of the like class in Amer
ica—that is to say, the coolie or the
laboring class —and the exclusion of
these is fully provided for by the laws
already in force. But the Chinese
nation decries with some heat the
practice of the United States to treat
as thieves, forgers and perjurors, and
to proclaim to the world that they are
such, that class of Chinese citizens
whose interests are personally mutual
with the same class of people in
America, and who have been decreed
to be entitled to free intercourse with
Americans by laws made hjy the peo
ple of this country themse.ves. By
these laws certain of the Chinese are
declared exempt from the harassing
examinations imposed upon the coolie
class on coming to this country, but
in practice the law is ignored, and
these ostensibly exempt visitors suf
fer indignities from which even lhe
coolie escapes. Why? Is it unrea
sonable to suppose that crooked in
spectors and bribe-taking under of
ficials consider them fatter pickings
than the coolies, and that personal ill
treatment is the quickest and surest
method to extort from them the cov
eted coin? This is not a violent sup
position, and instances may be cited
in attestation. One statement is fre
quently made and urged as a reason
for refusing to the merchants, stu
dents and travelers their rights under
the law and the amenities common
to civilized life. The rabid orators
and more insincere editors predict
that if the exempt class is admitted to
this country and treated as if they
were human beings, the coolie class
would take advantage of such a con
dition and flock hither in hordes,
armed with fraudulent certificates of
their standing as merchants, students,
etc.
It may be conceded that fraudulent
certificates —few in number, however
—have been used by dishonest China
men to gain entrance to this land.
But, it may be inquired, how do they

become possessed of these forged pa
pers? These documents are printed
in the English language, which
coolie is as unintelligible as are the
inscriptions on their own tea bozes to
the native of California; they are
stamped and marked, and have imita
tions of the consular vise; their en
tire appearance betokens their produc
duction by English-speaking peop.e.
When such imposing looking forged
documents are presented to the inno
cent and credulous coolie, accompa
nied by the usual exaggerated stories
of the fairyland to which they are
told these papers will take them, the
victim is easily entrapped, and the
wily and rapacious American “makes
good” to the amount of the deluded
coolie’s little store of cash. It is,
indubitably, the greedy American who
is the inspirer and promoter of these
rascalities, and the Ciprico case and
the later forgeries committed under
the roof of the custom house here in
dicate their origin and that even cus
tom officials are not without sin. If
it could be admitted—which it is not
—that fraudulent certificates are
used by the ignorant coolies in any
great numbers, it would be merely a
reflection upon this government that
it cannot discover or will not punish
its own criminals. It is submitted
that the merchants, teachers, stu
dents and travelers shall not be pun
ished for the crimes of others, and
that when they come to this country
for business, improvement or pleas
ure, they shall be treated with the ci
vility and courtesy which are shown
to Americans in China and which are
their right under American law. That
is all that China ask’s. President
Roosevelt’s views are right; his pol
icy is hfs own affair. •

Many Twin School Children.
There are fourteen pairs of twins in
the Whittier school, Minneapolis. Be
sides being in accord with President
Roosevelt as to race suicide, the
Whittier school district is strongly re
publican, having given 1,547 votes for
Roosevelt and 161 for Parker.
Chicago, Sept.—The Peruna Drug
Manufacturing Company, manufac
turers of a widely known proprietary
medicine, has brought suit in the Su
perior Court of the city of Chicago
against the Curtis Publishing Com
pany of Philadelphia, alleging that it
has been damaged to th«' extent of
$250,000 by a recent article in the
Ladies’ Home Journal.
The suit is based on a statement re
cently made in that journal that a tes
timonial as to the merit of the remedy
manufactured by the plaintiff, alleged
to have been given by Congressman
George H. White of South Carolina
was fraudulent, denial from Mr. White
that lie ever gave such a testimonial
also being printed.
The Peruna Company declares that
Congressman White did give the tes
timonial in good faith, that it has two
original letters from Mr. White. It
declares that Mr. White was led to
repudiate the testimonial through a
misunderstanding.
This is the second large damage suit
that has been filed against the Curtis
Publishing Company since it inaugu
rated its attacks on ‘’patent medi
cines.”

Bees are a Dissipated Lot.
•‘How does the busy bee improve
each shining hour?” He doesn’t.
Drake Wilson, who knows all about
bees, says that the bees rest winters,
just like a briCKlayer or lake sailor.
Not only that, but he asserts that the
big bees and the little bees can be
found drunk in the vineyards. They
sup the juice of the burst grapes until
they are full, then fall to the ground
and sleep off their debauch. They
even know enough not to go
home with a jag. And so always
goes the last vestige of the romance
that was supposed to be attached to
the beehives. —Chickasaw, I. T. Star.
Advancing the Farmers* Interests.
Traveling agents and salesmen are
now sent from the home offices of the
Chicago packers Into all South Ameri
can and Asiatic countries. They are
going into every 7 land, no matter what
language may be spoken or what
money be used. They will exchange
their goods for cowries or elephant
tusks—anything to sell the product
and get something in return converti
ble into money. It may seem odd to
some folks, but traveling men, carry
ing cases with samples of American
meat products, can be seen in the
desert of Sahara, the sands of Zanzi
bar or in Brazil, “where the nuts come
from.” Great is the enterprise of the
Yankee merchant. The greater the!
market, the greater the price and sta
bility of the price of the product and
all that goes to make it in its various
stages.

“Father of Nobody’s Children.”
The life work of Dr. Barnado, the
“father of nobody’s children,” the
rescuer of waifs, is likely to be made
a permanent institution. Eminent
men and women —statesmen, church
men of various denominations and
philanthropists—have started a move
ment for the collection of a fund
whose income shall be sufficient to
secure the permanency of Dr. Bar
nados benefleient organization when
the creator of it shall no longer be
able to direct it.
Dr. Barnado, who is certainly one o'
the great philanthropist of the age, is
sixty years old and in feeble health
For forty years he, almost alone ha
conducted a charity which has rescue
thousands of waifs annually with
practically no assured income. The
first home for destitute orphaned and
abandoned children which he organ
ized in the earliest days of his young
manhood has grown into an institution
which has converted multitudes of de
relict infants into useful men and
women. At the present moment he
has in his charge, in his various
homes, nearly nine thousand children,
whom he is feeding from day to day,
although his vested income amounts
to only $2,500 a year. For the rest
he depends wholly—and hitherto
never in vain—upon voluntary con
tributions. During the last few years
he has sent nearly seventeen thou
sand children to various British colo
nies, and of the whole number only
two per cent have failed to do well.
It is felt in England that such a life
work is worthy of something more
substantial than public admiration,
hence the movement to perpetuate his
work.

AGED BROTHERS SOUGHT
Middletown, bl. Y., Oct. 7.—Alice
Ingerick, nine years old, was
murdered in the cellar of a farmhouse
near here last night. Her mother,
Mrs. Georgia Ingerick, was later dis
covered in the barn suffering from
probably fatal wounds. The woman
kept house for Willis and Fred Olney,
two aged brothers. Search is being
made for them.
